1999.assessment:
No fiscal data for 1999. Economy and government finances completely
collapsed following post-referendum destruction. UNTAET assumed fiscal
administration. No national budget existed. International aid funding
all government functions.

2000.assessment:
UNTAET managed all fiscal functions. No national budget or national debt.
Funding entirely from international donors via UN Trust Fund. Some domestic
revenue collection beginning. Near-zero debt position as no borrowing had
occurred for the new state.

2001.assessment:
UNTAET fiscal year. Budget preparation beginning for independent government.
International Trust Fund managing finances. Very low debt position. Pre-
independence fiscal management being established. Revenue collection very limited.

2002.assessment:
First year of independent government fiscal management (from May 2002).
Revenue base very small — primarily donor grants and petroleum revenues.
Near-zero national debt. Budget dependent on international assistance.
Petroleum Fund concept being developed.

2003.assessment:
Fiscal situation improving as petroleum revenues from Timor Sea JDA growing.
Government budget small but manageable. Very low debt. International
assistance continuing. Petroleum Fund design finalized. Revenue from JPDA
flowing.

2004.assessment:
Fiscal situation building. Petroleum revenues from JPDA (Timor Sea joint
development area) flowing. Petroleum Fund to be established 2005. Budget
primarily funded by petroleum revenues and donor grants. Very low debt.

2005.assessment:
Excellent fiscal position. Petroleum Fund established 2005 — key institutional
milestone. Bayu-Undan petroleum revenues flowing strongly. Petroleum Fund
balance growing rapidly. Government debt very low. Budget primarily funded
by petroleum revenues. Sustainable withdrawal rate framework being developed.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2006.assessment:
Fiscal position strong despite 2006 political/military crisis. Petroleum
Fund accumulating revenues from Bayu-Undan. Crisis required some emergency
spending but petroleum revenues offset. Debt low. Petroleum Fund balance
growing toward $1 billion. Crisis managed fiscally.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2007.assessment:
Fiscal position good. Petroleum Fund growing rapidly (approaching $2 billion).
2007 elections established new Gusmao government with commitment to spending
from Petroleum Fund for development. Petroleum revenues from Bayu-Undan
at peak. Debt very low.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2008.assessment:
Strong fiscal position. Petroleum Fund growing rapidly (several billion
USD). Government spending accelerating for infrastructure. Excess above
sustainable withdrawal rate from Petroleum Fund. Petroleum revenues
exceptional year. Debt low. February assassination attempt contained.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2009.assessment:
Excellent fiscal position. Petroleum Fund approaching $5 billion. Global
financial crisis had minimal impact due to petroleum insulation. Government
spending from Fund driving economic growth. IMF commended fiscal management
but cautioned about excess withdrawals above sustainable rate.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2010.assessment:
Excellent fiscal position. Petroleum Fund approaching $7 billion. Government
spending large relative to non-oil GDP. IMF noted excess withdrawals above
sustainable rate. Infrastructure investment accelerating. Long-term
sustainability concerns if oil revenues decline.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2011.assessment:
Strong fiscal position. Petroleum Fund growing toward $10 billion (peak
several years later). Government spending very large relative to non-oil
GDP. Excess withdrawals above sustainable rate a growing concern. UNMIT
completing. Non-oil fiscal deficit large.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2012.assessment:
Petroleum Fund growing (>$10 billion). Peaceful 2012 elections. UNMIT
completed. Government spending large. Debt very low. Petroleum Fund
providing fiscal cushion but excess withdrawals above ESI (Estimated
Sustainable Income) rate concerning for long-term sustainability.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2013.assessment:
Petroleum Fund at or near peak (~$16 billion). Bayu-Undan production
at peak. Fiscal position strong but non-oil deficit very large relative
to non-oil GDP. Long-term fiscal cliff increasingly visible as Bayu-Undan
production set to decline. Greater Sunrise development critical.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2014.assessment:
Petroleum Fund still large but oil price collapse from mid-2014 reducing
revenues. Government maintaining high spending. Fiscal sustainability
concerns growing. IMF recommendations for fiscal consolidation. Debt low.
Withdrawal above sustainable rate accelerating.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2015.assessment:
Fiscal sustainability increasingly concerning. Oil price low, Bayu-Undan
production declining. Petroleum Fund declining. Non-oil fiscal deficit large.
Government reducing some spending but fiscal consolidation difficult
politically. Debt still low.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2016.assessment:
Fiscal sustainability concerns intensifying. Petroleum Fund declining from
peak. Oil revenues low. Government spending maintained. Non-oil fiscal
deficit requires Petroleum Fund drawdowns above sustainable rate. Debt
increasing slightly. Long-term fiscal cliff becoming urgent.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2017.assessment:
Fiscal situation complicated by political crisis — two election rounds
and budget impasse meant no approved budget for extended period. Government
spending constrained. This paradoxically reduced Petroleum Fund drawdowns.
Debt manageable. Long-term sustainability concerns dominant.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2018.assessment:
New ANC government resumed spending. Maritime boundary treaty with Australia
signed enabling Greater Sunrise development. Government borrowed some
from commercial sources for development. Debt increasing but still manageable.
Petroleum Fund declining but still substantial. Non-oil deficit large.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2019.assessment:
Fiscal sustainability concerns mounting. Petroleum Fund below $16B peak
and declining. Large non-oil fiscal deficit. Government borrowing for
Tasi Mane (onshore LNG) infrastructure project — major commitment.
Debt rising. Greater Sunrise development still uncertain.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2020.assessment:
Fiscal sustainability seriously concerning. Petroleum Fund declining rapidly.
COVID-19 added spending. Non-oil fiscal deficit extremely large. Government
borrowing for Tasi Mane. Debt rising. IMF strongly recommending fiscal
consolidation. FDI very negative (oil sector exit). Fiscal cliff imminent
without Greater Sunrise development.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2021.assessment:
Fiscal sustainability crisis. Petroleum Fund declining from ~$18B to lower
levels with excess withdrawals. Bayu-Undan nearing end of life. Non-oil
fiscal deficit massive. Government borrowing increasing. Tasi Mane
infrastructure project creating debt obligations. Debt reaching moderate
level. IMF strongly cautioning on fiscal trajectory.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2022.assessment:
Fiscal position worsening. Petroleum Fund depleted significantly below
peak. Bayu-Undan production effectively zero by 2023. Non-oil fiscal
deficit requires large Petroleum Fund drawdowns. Tasi Mane project creating
ongoing liabilities. Debt elevated but not yet unsustainable. Urgent need
for Greater Sunrise development or fiscal consolidation.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2023.assessment:
Fiscal sustainability at critical point. Bayu-Undan production ended.
Petroleum revenues near zero. Non-oil tax revenues insufficient. Government
dependent on Petroleum Fund drawdowns at unsustainable rates. Debt elevated.
ASEAN accession would not resolve fiscal cliff. Greater Sunrise development
now critical for fiscal sustainability.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2024.assessment:
Fiscal crisis. No petroleum revenues from Bayu-Undan. Petroleum Fund
declining to unsustainable levels. Government spending constrained. Debt
elevated. IMF recommending urgent fiscal adjustment. Greater Sunrise
development delays creating existential fiscal risk. Non-oil economy
growing but far too small to fill petroleum revenue gap.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV Consultations

2025.assessment:
Continuing fiscal sustainability crisis. Petroleum Fund declining. No
Bayu-Undan revenues. Greater Sunrise development critical but still delayed.
Fiscal adjustment underway but painful.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV estimates

1999.assessment:
GDP per capita at $270 — one of the lowest in the world and deeply depressed
by the Indonesian military and militia destruction following the independence
referendum in August-September 1999. Approximately 70-80% of infrastructure
destroyed. Economy effectively collapsed. UNTAET (UN Transitional Administration)
taking over administration. Population suffering extreme poverty.

2000.assessment:
GDP per capita rebounded to $492 as UNTAET administration established
and international aid flowed in. Recovery from 1999 destruction baseline
but still extremely low. Heavy dependency on international aid and UN
spending inflating GDP figures. Most infrastructure still being rebuilt.

2001.assessment:
GDP per capita $588 — continuing recovery under UNTAET. Constitutional
Assembly elected August 2001. International donor support strong. Economy
heavily aid-dependent. Petroleum production from Timor Sea fields beginning
to generate revenues. Still extremely poor by international standards.

2002.assessment:
GDP per capita $534 — slight decline from 2001 as UN peacekeeping spending
decreased with formal independence May 20, 2002. International aid continued
but transitioning to development assistance. New state had extremely limited
revenue base initially. Petroleum revenues increasing but not yet dominant.

2003.assessment:
GDP per capita stable at $535. Economy finding new baseline as international
UN mission presence stabilized. Petroleum Timor Sea revenues helping government
budget. Agriculture recovering. Unemployment high. Coffee sector recovering
as main non-oil export.

2004.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to $474 — some decline in UN mission spending as
UNMISET transitioned. Petroleum revenues from Timor Sea JDA growing but
Petroleum Fund not yet established. High aid dependency. Agriculture
dominated domestic economy for most population.

2005.assessment:
GDP per capita $487. Petroleum Fund established 2005 — major milestone for
long-term fiscal sustainability. Petroleum revenues from Bayu-Undan field
flowing strongly. Non-oil economy still very small. Major governance milestone
with Petroleum Fund ensuring future revenues managed transparently.

2006.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to $465 — 2006 political/military crisis severely
disrupted economy. Gang violence in Dili caused internal displacement of
~150,000 people. Investment halted. Security crisis required Australian-led
intervention (ISF). Economic activity suppressed by violence and uncertainty.

2007.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to $540 following 2006 crisis stabilization. Elections
held June-July 2007 with Ramos-Horta as President and AMP coalition under
Gusmao as PM. Petroleum Fund revenues growing — Petroleum Fund balance
reaching several hundred million USD. Government spending increasing significantly.

2008.assessment:
GDP per capita $628. Assassination attempt on President Ramos-Horta in
February 2008 did not derail economic progress. Petroleum Fund growing to
over $3 billion. Government spending from petroleum revenues driving
growth. Non-oil GDP growing substantially. Economy on positive trajectory.

2009.assessment:
GDP per capita $687. Non-oil economy growing at double-digit rates driven
by government spending from petroleum revenues. Infrastructure investment
accelerating. Petroleum Fund balance growing. Global financial crisis had
minimal direct impact due to petroleum revenue insulation.

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita $813. Significant improvement driven by petroleum revenue
spending. Non-oil economy growing rapidly. Poverty declining. Petroleum Fund
approaching $7 billion. Major infrastructure projects underway. Security
situation stabilizing with UNMIT mission ongoing.

2011.assessment:
GDP per capita $939. Continued strong growth from government petroleum revenue
spending. Non-oil economy expanding in construction, services, and trade.
Petroleum Fund exceeding $10 billion. UNMIT mission completed December 2012.
Living standards gradually improving from very low base.

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita surpassed $1,000 milestone. Peaceful and successful presidential
and parliamentary elections in 2012 — major stability milestone. UNMIT mission
completed December 2012. Economy passed $1,000 per capita for first time.
Petroleum Fund peak around $16+ billion. Growing economy with stability.

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita $1,205. Post-UNMIT period of independent consolidation.
Government spending from Petroleum Fund driving growth. Bayu-Undan field
at peak production. Timor Sea Maritime Boundary negotiations with Australia
ongoing. Economy expanding but heavily dependent on petroleum revenues.

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita $1,225 — near-peak in non-oil terms. Petroleum revenues still
strong. Non-oil economy developing. Poverty reduction progressing. Maritime
boundary negotiations with Australia continued. Greater Sunrise field development
becoming critical issue for long-term fiscal sustainability.

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita $1,320 — near historical peak. However, oil price collapse
2014-2016 reducing petroleum revenues and Petroleum Fund withdrawals becoming
unsustainable. Bayu-Undan field production declining. Long-term fiscal
sustainability concerns emerging. Non-oil economy insufficient to compensate.

2016.assessment:
GDP per capita at historical peak in non-oil terms $1,336. Petroleum Fund
drawdowns accelerating. Fiscal concerns mounting. Non-oil GDP growing but
not enough to replace petroleum revenues. Political stability maintained
ahead of 2017 elections. Ramos-Horta not seeking re-election.

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to $1,266. Two election cycles in 2017 — parliamentary
elections July 2017 won by FRETILIN (minority government), then second elections
called. Political deadlock as ANC could not agree coalition. Government unable
to pass budget. Economic uncertainty from political instability.

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita $1,219 — declining as political crisis continued. ANC coalition
under Gusmao finally formed government May 2018 but political instability
impacted investment. Petroleum Fund declining. TLEA (Timor-Leste Enabling Act)
for maritime boundary with Australia signed. Bayu-Undan declining production.

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita jumped to $1,562 — significant increase partly reflecting
statistical revisions and Greater Sunrise field revenue expectations. ASEAN
membership formal application submitted 2011, accession still pending. Fiscal
situation improving with renewed investment interest in Greater Sunrise.

2020.assessment:
GDP per capita $1,631 despite COVID-19 pandemic. East Timor's relatively
isolated economy insulated somewhat from global disruption. Government
spending maintained from Petroleum Fund. Political instability continued
with fractious coalition politics. ASEAN accession still unresolved.

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita surged to $2,685 — dramatic jump reflecting statistical
revisions incorporating Timor Sea petroleum production in national accounts
more comprehensively. This represents a methodological revision, not purely
organic growth. Real economic conditions improved moderately with COVID recovery.

2022.assessment:
GDP per capita $2,343 — still elevated but declining from 2021 peak.
Bayu-Undan field near end of life. Greater Sunrise development delays.
Long-term fiscal sustainability becoming critical issue as petroleum revenues
declining. Non-oil economic development still insufficient. Political stability
improved with Gusmao returning as PM.

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita declined to $1,502 — significant drop reflecting end of
Bayu-Undan production and reduced petroleum revenues. Fiscal pressure
intensifying. Non-oil economy unable to compensate. ASEAN membership
still pending (admitted 2023 in principle, formal accession ongoing).
Long-term fiscal cliff looming without Greater Sunrise development.

2024.assessment:
GDP per capita declined further to $1,332. Post-Bayu-Undan petroleum revenue
decline ongoing. Fiscal sustainability crisis intensifying. Government drawing
down Petroleum Fund below sustainable withdrawal rate. Greater Sunrise
development still delayed. Non-oil economy developing slowly. Long-term
economic viability dependent on Greater Sunrise or alternative development.

2025.assessment:
GDP declining due to continued petroleum revenue fall. Non-oil economy
growing but insufficient to compensate for petroleum sector decline.
Fiscal sustainability remains critical concern.

1999.assessment:
No reliable inflation data for 1999. Economy was effectively destroyed
following post-referendum violence. Price data not collected. East Timor
used the Indonesian rupiah and then transitioned to US dollar as official
currency under UNTAET from early 2000.

2000.assessment:
Inflation data unreliable. USD adopted as official currency in early 2000
under UNTAET, eliminating currency risk but creating imported inflation.
Reconstruction activities driving some price increases. No comprehensive
price index yet established.

2001.assessment:
Early price data collection beginning under UNTAET. Inflation estimated
moderate as economy was building from near-zero base. Construction and
services inflation driven by UN mission spending. USD currency anchored
prices. No official CPI established yet.

2002.assessment:
Post-independence inflation moderate. USD currency maintained price stability.
UNTAET/UNMISET spending winding down could reduce some price pressures.
Very early state of statistical systems — formal CPI just being established.

2003.assessment:
CPI inflation 7.2% — elevated but not crisis level. USD currency limiting
monetary inflation but food and fuel prices elevated. New state establishing
statistical infrastructure including formal price indices.

2004.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 3.2%. USD currency providing stability. Economy
stabilizing. Food prices more stable. Petroleum revenues beginning to flow
into government budget.

2005.assessment:
Very low inflation at 1.1%. USD currency maintaining price stability.
Petroleum Fund established 2005 providing fiscal cushion. Economic conditions
broadly stable.

2006.assessment:
Inflation rose to 3.9% as 2006 political/military crisis disrupted
supply chains. Food prices increased with displacement of ~150,000 people
in Dili. Security crisis adding cost pressures.

2007.assessment:
Inflation jumped to 10.3% due to global food and fuel price spike plus
post-2006 crisis reconstruction demand. Government spending from petroleum
revenues increasing. Supply bottlenecks. Global commodity price boom
2007-2008 significantly affecting this import-dependent economy.

2008.assessment:
Inflation 9.1% — still elevated from global commodity price pressures.
Government spending from petroleum revenues strong. February 2008 assassination
attempt on Ramos-Horta did not significantly impact price stability.

2009.assessment:
Inflation collapsed to 0.7% as global commodity prices fell dramatically
following 2008 financial crisis. USD currency absorbed global deflation.
Food and fuel import prices fell sharply. Excellent price stability.

2010.assessment:
Inflation returned to 6.8% as global commodity prices recovered and
government spending from petroleum revenues increased strongly. Infrastructure
investment creating demand-side pressures. Food prices rising globally.

2011.assessment:
Inflation spiked to 13.5% — highest recorded. Combined effect of global
food price crisis 2011, strong government spending from petroleum revenues,
and supply-side constraints. Import-dependent economy vulnerable to global
price shocks. USD providing no monetary policy tool to respond.

2012.assessment:
Inflation 11.8% — still elevated but declining from 2011 peak. Successful
elections in 2012 maintaining stability. Government spending continued strong.
Global food prices moderating somewhat. UNMIT mission completed December 2012.

2013.assessment:
Inflation 11.1% — persistently elevated. Strong government spending maintained
demand pressures. Import prices elevated globally. East Timor's import-
dependent economy structurally exposed. Infrastructure investment contributing
to sustained price pressure.

2014.assessment:
Inflation collapsed to 0.7% — dramatic decline from 2011-2013 elevated
levels. Global commodity price decline 2014 contributed. USD currency
transmitted global deflation. Oil price collapse from mid-2014 reduced
import costs. Excellent price stability.

2015.assessment:
Very low inflation at 0.6%. Continued global commodity price weakness.
Oil prices remained low. USD currency providing stability. Government
spending from Petroleum Fund declining slightly.

2016.assessment:
Deflation at -1.3% — prices actually falling. USD transmitted global
deflationary pressures. Low oil and commodity prices persisting. Government
spending from Petroleum Fund declining as revenues fell.

2017.assessment:
Very low inflation at 0.6%. Political crisis (two election rounds, budget
impasse) reduced government spending and demand. USD stability maintained.
Low global commodity prices continuing.

2018.assessment:
Inflation returned to 2.6% — moderate. New ANC government under Gusmao
formed May 2018, government spending resumed more normally. Global oil
prices recovering. Economy stabilizing.

2019.assessment:
Very low inflation at 1.0%. Global commodity prices stable. USD currency
maintaining stability. Government spending constrained by fiscal sustainability
concerns.

2020.assessment:
Near-zero inflation at 0.5%. COVID-19 pandemic suppressed demand. Global
oil and commodity prices collapsed temporarily in 2020. USD transmitted
deflationary global conditions.

2021.assessment:
Inflation rose to 3.7% as global supply chain disruptions post-COVID pushed
prices higher. Global commodity price recovery. East Timor's import-dependent
economy feeling global inflationary pressures transmitted through USD and
import prices. Moderate but manageable.

2022.assessment:
Inflation rose to 7.0% driven by Russia-Ukraine war food and energy price
shock. Highly import-dependent economy exposed to global price increases.
Food price inflation particularly significant for poor population.

2023.assessment:
Inflation 8.4% — elevated. Continuing global inflationary spillover.
East Timor's import dependence maintaining price pressure. Fiscal spending
on infrastructure contributing to domestic demand. Food inflation significant.

2024.assessment:
Inflation moderated significantly to 2.1% as global inflationary pressures
eased. Global food and energy prices declining. USD providing stability.
Price environment benign.

2025.assessment:
Low to moderate inflation expected continuing benign global price environment.
Import-dependent economy sensitive to global commodity prices.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV estimates

1999.assessment:
No trade data for 1999. Post-referendum destruction effectively ended all
formal trade. Economy collapsed with infrastructure destroyed. Any remaining
trade was informal or aid-related. No systematic trade statistics.

2000.assessment:
Extraordinary trade openness at 176% of GDP — massively inflated by
UN/donor aid flows treated as imports. UNTAET bringing in massive amounts
of goods, food, equipment, and services. GDP denominator extremely small
making ratio very high. Not representative of normal economic integration.
Economy entirely dependent on foreign inputs.

2001.assessment:
Very high trade ratio 167% — still dominated by UN mission-related imports.
Exports minimal (coffee main export). Economy heavily import-dependent.
Constitutional Assembly established in August 2001. International aid
flows continuing at high level through UNTAET.

2002.assessment:
Peak trade ratio 180% — formal independence May 2002 with high aid/import
flows. GDP denominator still very small. UNTAET transitioned to UNMISET.
Exports extremely limited: primarily coffee and some petroleum products.
Economy completely import-dependent.

2003.assessment:
Trade ratio declining to 134% as UN mission presence stabilized and
GDP grew. Still very high from import dependence. Petroleum revenues
beginning to flow. Coffee exports resuming. Aid still a major component
of imports. Economy developing slowly.

2004.assessment:
Trade ratio 112% — still highly open. Imports declining as UN mission
footprint reduces. GDP growing modestly. Export base still very narrow
(coffee, petroleum). Import dependence structural for this small island
economy.

2005.assessment:
Trade ratio fell below 100% to 99%. Petroleum Fund established 2005.
GDP growing as petroleum revenues included. Imports/exports more balanced
in ratio terms as GDP denominator grows. Economy still highly import-dependent
but normalizing.

2006.assessment:
Trade ratio 77%. 2006 political/military crisis reduced formal trade
activity. Investment declined during crisis. GDP denominator growing.
Economy still open but less than previous years due to crisis disruption.

2007.assessment:
Trade ratio 73%. Post-2006 recovery. Elections in 2007 restored stability.
Government spending increased. Import activity recovering. Economy open
by regional standards for small developing country.

2008.assessment:
Trade ratio 78%. Government spending driving imports. Construction and
infrastructure imports increasing. Coffee exports performing well at high
global prices. Economy growing strongly. Assassination attempt on President
Ramos-Horta in February 2008 contained.

2009.assessment:
Trade ratio 85%. Strong government spending maintained imports. Global
financial crisis not significantly impacting East Timor due to petroleum
revenue insulation. Construction and services imports growing with
infrastructure investment.

2010.assessment:
Trade ratio back near 100% at 98%. Strong government expenditure driving
large import volumes. Infrastructure investment at peak. GDP growing but
imports growing faster. Economy very open and import-dependent.

2011.assessment:
Trade ratio 87%. GDP growing rapidly with strong petroleum revenues. Import
levels high from government spending. Non-oil exports limited. Economy
open. UNMIT mission completing. Infrastructure investment continuing.

2012.assessment:
Trade ratio 77%. Successful 2012 elections and political stability. UNMIT
completed December 2012. Economy normalizing. GDP growing. Trade ratio
stabilizing at moderate-open level. Economy open for small developing country.

2013.assessment:
Trade ratio declined to 66% as GDP grew faster than imports. Post-UNMIT
period with normalizing aid flows. Government spending from Petroleum Fund
still high. Non-oil exports (coffee, some petroleum) limited. Economy
moderately open.

2014.assessment:
Trade ratio 61%. Oil price collapse from mid-2014 beginning to affect
import capacity. GDP still growing. Non-oil exports limited. Economy
moderately open. Greater Sunrise negotiation a key issue.

2015.assessment:
Trade ratio 59%. Low oil prices reducing government revenues and capacity
for import-intensive investments. GDP still moderate. Trade at moderate
level typical for small Pacific-adjacent economy.

2016.assessment:
Trade ratio 66%. Economy stable. Low oil prices continued. Government
seeking to diversify. ASEAN membership application active (applied 2011).
Trade moderately open by regional standards.

2017.assessment:
Trade ratio 72%. Political crisis — two election rounds and budget impasse.
Despite political uncertainty, trade flows relatively maintained. Import-
dependence structural. Economy open by regional LDC standards.

2018.assessment:
Trade ratio 76%. New ANC government formed May 2018, government spending
resumed. Maritime boundary treaty with Australia signed enabling Greater
Sunrise development. Import activity recovering with government budget
normalization.

2019.assessment:
Trade ratio jumped to 90% — significant increase in trade activity.
Government infrastructure spending resumed fully. Import-intensive investment
projects. FDI strongly negative (oil sector exit by ConocoPhillips). GDP
also larger from statistical revisions. Economy open.

2020.assessment:
Trade ratio 85%. COVID-19 pandemic slightly reduced trade activity but
government spending from Petroleum Fund maintained imports. Tourism (small)
collapsed. Trade with Indonesia (dominant trading partner) somewhat disrupted.

2021.assessment:
Trade ratio 80%. Post-COVID recovery. Large statistical revision inflated
GDP denominator, reducing ratio somewhat. Government spending maintained.
ASEAN accession progress. Economy rebuilding trade relations.

2022.assessment:
Trade ratio 85%. Economy open. Russia-Ukraine global price shock increasing
import bill. Government spending from Petroleum Fund. Gusmao returning as PM.
Economy continuing to be import-dominated with limited export diversification.

2023.assessment:
Trade ratio 95% — approaching highly open. GDP declining from petroleum
revenue fall while imports maintained from Petroleum Fund drawdowns.
Import-dependent economy fully exposed to external market. ASEAN accession
would improve trade access. FDI recovering.

2024.assessment:
Trade ratio estimated near 100% as GDP declined further with petroleum
revenues falling while import levels maintained from Petroleum Fund. Highly
import-dependent economy. ASEAN accession formally accepted in principle 2023.
Long-term trade diversification needed.

2025.assessment:
Economy continues to be highly import-dependent. ASEAN accession would
improve trade integration. Long-term trade diversification a priority.

sources.citation: IMF East Timor Article IV estimates

1999.assessment:
No reliable unemployment data for 1999. Post-referendum destruction left
the vast majority of the population without formal employment. Most
economic activity collapsed. Survival agriculture and subsistence the norm.
Formal labor market statistics non-existent.

sources.citation: UNDP East Timor Human Development Reports

2000.assessment:
No formal unemployment data. Economy being rebuilt under UNTAET. Most
population engaged in subsistence agriculture. Formal sector employment
very limited — primarily UN mission staff and early government employees.
ILO estimates place effective unemployment and underemployment very high.

2001.assessment:
Formal employment still very limited. Constitutional Assembly elections
August 2001 creating some government employment. UN mission providing
employment. Formal unemployment statistics still not established. Youth
unemployment particularly high in Dili.

2002.assessment:
Post-independence formal labor market very small. Government employment
expanding. Private sector very limited. ILO estimated unemployment rates
high due to limited formal sector. Subsistence agriculture dominant. Labor
force surveys not yet systematically conducted.

2003.assessment:
ILO modeled unemployment estimate ~3.6% — very low by ILO methodology which
counts anyone doing any work including subsistence farming as employed.
Real underemployment and informal sector vulnerability much higher. This
figure reflects ILO definitional approach rather than job quality conditions.
Most employment is subsistence agriculture, informal, or under-employed.

2004.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.5%. Very low formal unemployment by ILO definition.
Economy stabilizing. Agriculture dominant. Formal private sector still very
small. Government employment the main formal sector employer.

2005.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.4%. Petroleum Fund established 2005 beginning to
channel petroleum revenues into government. Formal employment growing
slowly. Subsistence agriculture still dominant. Youth unemployment in
urban areas high but not captured in ILO estimates.

2006.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.4%. 2006 political/military crisis disrupted
urban employment particularly in Dili. 150,000 IDPs lost livelihoods
temporarily. Real unemployment conditions worsened during crisis period
but ILO modeled estimate doesn't capture this disruption well.

2007.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.3%. Post-crisis recovery. 2007 elections brought
new government under Gusmao. Government spending increased significantly
from petroleum revenues — creating construction employment and government
jobs. Economy recovering from 2006 crisis.

2008.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.3%. Strong government spending driving employment.
Construction boom in Dili and infrastructure. February 2008 assassination
attempt on Ramos-Horta contained — did not derail employment conditions.
Petroleum Fund resources enabling large government employment programs.

2009.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.1%. Global financial crisis had limited direct
impact due to petroleum revenue insulation. Government spending maintained.
Construction and public sector employment continuing. Economy growing at
double-digit rates driven by government expenditure.

2010.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.0%. Employment conditions improving with economic
growth. Construction sector major employer. Government civil service expanding.
UNMIT mission employment winding down. Formal private sector still small
but growing.

2011.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~3.0%. Economy growing strongly. Employment in
construction, government, and services expanding. UNMIT mission completed
December 2012. Poverty levels still high despite formal employment figures.
Labor force surveys showing high underemployment.

2012.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.9%. Successful 2012 elections. UNMIT mission
completed December 2012. Economy and employment conditions stable.
Government expenditure-driven employment continued. Private sector
investment beginning with political stability.

2013.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.8%. Economy growing. Government spending from
Petroleum Fund maintaining employment. Petroleum Fund approaching peak.
Concern about long-term fiscal sustainability and post-petroleum employment.

2014.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.8%. Economy stable. Oil price collapse from
mid-2014 beginning to reduce petroleum revenues. Long-term fiscal
sustainability concerns. Private sector still small. Youth unemployment
structurally high despite low ILO headline number.

2015.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.7%. Low oil prices reducing petroleum revenues.
Government spending tapering. Employment growth slowing. Private sector
development still insufficient to replace petroleum-driven public spending.
Structural unemployment concerns for youth.

2016.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.6%. Low oil price environment reducing fiscal
space. Government seeking to diversify economy. Private sector very small.
Economy facing long-term structural challenge as petroleum revenues decline.

2017.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.5%. Political crisis — two election rounds and
budget impasse reduced government spending significantly. Real employment
conditions likely worsened despite low ILO headline figure. Government
unable to implement full budget.

2018.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.4%. New ANC government formed May 2018, spending
resumed. Maritime boundary treaty with Australia signed, enabling Greater
Sunrise field development progress. Employment recovering from 2017 political
uncertainty.

2019.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.3%. Economy growing with government spending. Private
sector development ongoing. Greater Sunrise field development progressing.
FDI strongly negative in 2019 (oil sector exit). Government employment
dominant formal sector employer.

2020.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~2.1%. COVID-19 pandemic limited impact due to
small formal sector and agricultural-dominant livelihoods. Government
spending from Petroleum Fund maintained. Tourism (small but growing) hit.
Overall formal employment broadly maintained.

2021.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~1.9%. Post-COVID recovery. Government spending
maintained from Petroleum Fund. Bayu-Undan field declining but not yet
zero. ASEAN accession formal application. FDI recovered from large negative.

2022.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~1.8%. Economy recovering. Gusmao returned as PM.
Government spending from Petroleum Fund despite declining revenues. Fiscal
pressure mounting as Bayu-Undan production declining. Long-term employment
sustainability concerns.

2023.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~1.6%. Bayu-Undan production effectively ended.
Petroleum revenues declining sharply. Government spending constrained.
ASEAN accession continuing. Long-term fiscal and employment sustainability
critical issue without Greater Sunrise development.

2024.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate ~1.5%. Very low formal unemployment by ILO definition
but this masks persistent underemployment and informal sector precarity.
Fiscal pressure from petroleum revenue decline. Non-oil economy developing
slowly. Long-term structural challenge.

2025.assessment:
Low formal unemployment by ILO estimates but persistent underemployment.
Fiscal pressure from petroleum revenue decline a structural challenge for
employment creation.

1999.assessment:
No formal diplomatic recognition as independent state — East Timor was
still legally under Indonesian sovereignty until October 1999 (UNTAET
UNSC 1272). However, international community unanimously supported
independence. UNTAET transitional administration under UN. Future state
had unprecedented international goodwill.

2000.assessment:
UNTAET administering territory. No formal diplomatic relations as state
does not formally exist yet. International community fully engaged with
trust fund and development support. Pre-recognition period with broad
international commitment.

2001.assessment:
Pre-independence period with growing diplomatic contacts. Constitutional
Assembly elections internationally observed. Many states preparing for
future diplomatic relations. CNRM/CNRT diplomatic network active.
Full transition toward independence with wide international support.

2002.assessment:
Full diplomatic recognition following independence May 20, 2002.
UN membership admitted May 27, 2002 as 191st member. Dozens of states
immediately recognized East Timor. Portugal, Australia, US, Indonesia
among early recognizers. Very strong diplomatic integration for new state.
Also joined IMF, World Bank, ASDB immediately.

2003.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Extensive bilateral relations. UNMISET
continuing. International development assistance. Joined Community of
Portuguese Language Countries (CPLP). Active diplomatic presence in
multiple capitals.

2004.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Bilateral embassies in Dili. Timor-Leste
diplomatic missions in key capitals. Member of UN system agencies.
Petroleum Fund negotiations with Australia a key diplomatic activity.
Strong donor relations.

2005.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Petroleum Fund established. CMATS maritime
treaty with Australia signed (Treaty on Certain Maritime Arrangements
in the Timor Sea). Active bilateral relations. UNOTIL now replaced UNMISET.
Diplomatic capacity growing.

2006.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration during crisis. Crisis attracted international
support — Australia, NZ, Portugal, Malaysia deployed ISF. International
community highly engaged. UN authorized UNMIT. No diplomatic isolation.
Crisis management with full international support.

2007.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. UNMIT deployed. Elections internationally
observed. Ramos-Horta (Nobel Peace Prize laureate) as President — strong
international recognition. Gusmao as PM with global political connections.
Extensive bilateral and multilateral engagement.

2008.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Assassination attempt on Ramos-Horta
drew international sympathy. UNMIT and ISF continuing. Strong bilateral
relations. Petroleum Fund growing creating diplomatic opportunities.

2009.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. UNMIT continuing. Development assistance
active. Bilateral relations functioning. ASEAN exploratory engagement.
Timor-Leste engaged with Pacific and Asian regional forums.

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. UNMIT winding down. ASEAN Observer status
obtained. Ramos-Horta active internationally. Developing bilateral
relations broadly. Preparing ASEAN full membership application.

2011.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. ASEAN full membership application submitted.
Active bilateral relations with all major powers. Pacific regional
engagement. UNMIT completing. Timor-Leste active in international forums.

2012.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. UNMIT completed December 2012 — milestone
in Timor-Leste's international standing. Praised globally for democratic
election success. ASEAN accession proceeding. International development
partnerships.

2013.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Post-UNMIT era. Independent bilateral
relations. Active engagement in Pacific regional forums. ASEAN accession
consideration. International development assistance. Full diplomatic standing.

2014.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. CMATS maritime agreement under question.
Active bilateral relations. ASEAN accession ongoing. International
development and trade partnerships. Good standing in UN system.

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Timor-Leste initiated UNCLOS arbitration
against Australia regarding maritime spying claim. This demonstrated
ability to use international legal mechanisms — sign of full integration.
Active bilateral and multilateral relations.

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Maritime boundary arbitration with Australia
continuing. CMATS terminated 2017. Active bilateral relations. ASEAN
accession ongoing. Timor-Leste engaged with UN sustainable development goals.

2017.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Timor-Leste terminated CMATS and initiated
compulsory conciliation with Australia under UNCLOS. Successfully concluded
March 2018. Political crisis domestic only — international standing
maintained. Active ASEAN candidacy.

2018.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Maritime boundary treaty signed with Australia
March 6, 2018 — landmark achievement settling Timor Sea boundary. Enables
Greater Sunrise field development. Ramos-Horta praised internationally.
ASEAN accession advancing. Strong bilateral and multilateral relations.

2019.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Maritime boundary treaty ratified. ASEAN
accession continuing. Development partnerships. Bilateral relations active.
Good international standing. Petroleum Fund providing diplomatic flexibility.

2020.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. COVID-19 international support including
vaccine cooperation. ASEAN engagement. Bilateral relations maintained.
International development partnerships continued.

2021.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. ASEAN accession advancing significantly.
Active bilateral relations. Timor-Leste's democratic credentials strengthening
international standing. Ramos-Horta increasingly active internationally.

2022.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. ASEAN admitted Timor-Leste in principle at
November 2022 Phnom Penh summit. Ramos-Horta returned as President (April 2022)
bringing vast international experience. Gusmao PM with global relationships.
Very strong international standing.

sources.citation: ASEAN in principle November 2022; Ramos-Horta President

2023.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. ASEAN accession formal process ongoing after
2022 in-principle admission. Active bilateral relations. Ramos-Horta
active diplomatic agenda. Timor-Leste well-integrated in international
community for 21-year-old state.

2024.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. ASEAN accession formal completion anticipated.
Active bilateral and multilateral relations. Timor-Leste is among the
most diplomatically integrated small states in Southeast Asia given its
unique history and the profiles of its leadership.

2025.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration expected. ASEAN formal membership likely.
Bilateral and multilateral relations strong.

1999.assessment:
No FDI data for 1999. Economy was destroyed following post-referendum
violence. No investment activity possible. Investors fled. Economy
entirely dependent on international aid and UNTAET administration.

2000.assessment:
Very minimal FDI. UNTAET administering territory. Some early reconstruction
business activity but formal FDI negligible. UN administration contracting
not counted as FDI. First investors beginning to arrive.

2001.assessment:
Pre-independence FDI minimal. Constitutional Assembly establishing
basic governance. Oil companies beginning negotiations for Timor Sea
petroleum contracts. Tourism and service sector investors exploratory.
Formal FDI very limited.

2002.assessment:
Post-independence FDI gradually increasing. Independence May 2002 opened
formal investment environment. Petroleum sector beginning formal FDI.
Retail and service sector investment in Dili. Still very small formal
investment environment.

2003.assessment:
FDI 0.92% of GDP — minimal to very low. Petroleum sector investments
(Bayu-Undan field development by ConocoPhillips) the dominant FDI.
Non-petroleum private investment very small. Economy still entirely
aid-dependent with limited private sector.

2004.assessment:
FDI 0.24% of GDP — minimal. Petroleum investment declining post-Bayu-Undan
initial development. Non-petroleum private investment very limited.
Small economy with limited private sector development. UNMISET ending May 2005.

2005.assessment:
FDI 0.20% — minimal. Petroleum Fund established 2005 beginning to channel
revenues. Bayu-Undan in production. UNOTIL advisory. Non-petroleum
private investment very small. Investment climate improving but still
very limited.

2006.assessment:
FDI 1.87% — low but increased. Despite 2006 political/military crisis,
petroleum sector investment continued. Bayu-Undan oil/gas production
operating. Non-petroleum investment negligible during crisis period.
Crisis year but petroleum FDI sustained.

2007.assessment:
FDI 1.6% — low. Post-2006 crisis recovery. New government under Gusmao
bringing investor confidence. Petroleum sector FDI (Bayu-Undan continuing).
Some non-petroleum investment starting in construction and services.

2008.assessment:
FDI 6.12% — high. Surge in petroleum sector FDI. ConocoPhillips and
other companies investing in Bayu-Undan operations. Strong government
spending creating business opportunities. Non-petroleum investment
growing. Despite assassination attempt on Ramos-Horta, FDI surged.

2009.assessment:
FDI 6.6% — highest recorded. Petroleum sector investments peak. GFC
limited impact due to petroleum insulation. Bayu-Undan field operations
at full production. Government spending creating business environment.
Strong non-petroleum investment in construction and services.

2010.assessment:
FDI 3.44% — moderate. Petroleum FDI declining from peak but still
significant. Government spending creating strong business environment.
Non-petroleum investment in construction, retail, hospitality growing.
Strong economic growth attracting investors.

2011.assessment:
FDI 4.71% — moderate-high. Growing business confidence. Government
spending stimulus attracting investment. Construction boom. Petroleum
sector FDI continuing. UNMIT completing. Non-petroleum investment
in services, retail, banking growing.

2012.assessment:
FDI 3.48% — moderate. Successful 2012 elections boosting investor
confidence. UNMIT completed December 2012 — security stability
message positive. Post-petroleum peak but still healthy FDI level.

2013.assessment:
FDI 4.0% — moderate. Post-UNMIT independent governance attracting
investors. Petroleum sector FDI continuing. Non-petroleum investment
growing slowly. Infrastructure projects attracting international
construction companies.

2014.assessment:
FDI 2.34% — moderate but declining. Oil price collapse from mid-2014
reducing petroleum sector investment. Government spending tapering.
Non-petroleum investment modest. Bayu-Undan declining production
reducing petroleum FDI.

2015.assessment:
FDI 2.70% — moderate. Despite low oil prices, some investment continued.
Government infrastructure programs attracting foreign construction investors.
Non-petroleum investment (hospitality, retail) modest. Overall FDI
moderate by Southeast Asian LDC standards.

2016.assessment:
FDI collapsed to 0.33% — minimal. Low oil prices, declining Bayu-Undan
production, and reduced government spending all suppressed investment.
Non-petroleum private investment also declined. Political uncertainty
ahead of 2017 elections adding investor uncertainty.

2017.assessment:
FDI 0.42% — still minimal. Political crisis (two election rounds, budget
impasse) severely impacted investment. No approved budget for extended
period. Investors waited for political resolution. Non-petroleum investment
particularly suppressed.

2018.assessment:
FDI 3.08% — moderate recovery. New ANC government formed May 2018.
Maritime boundary treaty signed March 2018 enabling Greater Sunrise
development prospects. Government spending resumed. Investor confidence
recovering. Petroleum-adjacent investments increasing.

2019.assessment:
FDI turned sharply negative at -7.79% — major oil sector divestment.
ConocoPhillips sold its 30% stake in Greater Sunrise/JPDA assets to
Timor-Leste state company TIMOR GAP. This large transfer is counted as
negative FDI outflow. Not a loss of confidence per se — strategic
repatriation of petroleum assets to national control.

2020.assessment:
FDI deeply negative at -16.21% — largest negative FDI recorded. Continued
oil sector repatriation (Shell and others selling JPDA stakes). COVID-19
also suppressed new investment. These large negative numbers reflect
petroleum company exits/sales rather than collapsing investment environment.
TIMOR GAP acquiring stakes.

2021.assessment:
FDI -6.59% — still negative from petroleum repatriation continuing. Post-COVID
recovery beginning. TIMOR GAP now major player in petroleum sector. Non-
petroleum FDI recovering. Ongoing oil sector restructuring driving negative
aggregate figure.

2022.assessment:
FDI 1.67% — returned to positive. Petroleum sector repatriation cycle
largely completed. New non-petroleum investment. Gusmao as PM bringing
confidence. ASEAN accession in principle in November 2022 attracting
investors. Recovery to positive FDI.

2023.assessment:
FDI 5.87% — high. Strong recovery. Greater Sunrise development interest
generating investment. ASEAN accession attracting regional investors.
Petroleum sector restructuring completed enabling new investment cycle.
Non-petroleum investment in hospitality, retail, services also growing.

2024.assessment:
FDI 11.56% — very high. Major investment surge partly reflecting Greater
Sunrise field development activities. ASEAN accession. Petroleum sector
new investment cycle. This may reflect TIMOR GAP/partner investments in
Greater Sunrise project. Very strong FDI despite fiscal challenges at
government level.

2025.assessment:
FDI expected to remain elevated with Greater Sunrise development.
ASEAN membership supporting regional investment. Petroleum sector
restructuring complete.

1999.assessment:
Australian-led INTERFET (International Force for East Timor) deployed
September 20, 1999 under UN Security Council Resolution 1264 to halt
TNI/militia violence. Led by Australian Lt Gen Peter Cosgrove. Approximately
11,000 personnel from 22 countries. Essential and invited force —
East Timorese leadership actively requested international intervention.
Indonesian military withdrawing. This is invited significant foreign military presence.

2000.assessment:
UNTAET military component under PKF (Peacekeeping Force) replaced INTERFET
February 2000. UNTAET PKF approximately 8,950 troops (including force from
Australia, Bangladesh, Jordan, Philippines, etc.). Full UN peacekeeping
force essential for security. Administration and military both UN under
SRSG Sérgio Vieira de Mello.

2001.assessment:
UNTAET PKF approximately 6,281 troops continuing. Security responsibility
transferring toward East Timorese institutions (PNTL, F-FDTL being established).
UN military component still dominant. Pre-independence period with invited
UN peacekeeping force.

2002.assessment:
UNMISET (UN Mission of Support in East Timor) replaced UNTAET at
independence May 20, 2002. UNMISET initially ~5,000 troops and police.
Invited UN security force. East Timorese F-FDTL and PNTL gradually
assuming more functions. Transition from UNTAET to UNMISET.

2003.assessment:
UNMISET military component reducing — approximately 3,000+ troops.
Transition toward East Timorese security force independence ongoing.
Some bilateral military assistance from Australia, Portugal. UN military
presence still significant but declining.

2004.assessment:
UNMISET military component reducing toward May 2005 end date.
Approximately 700-1,000 military personnel remaining. Transition planning.
F-FDTL growing. PNTL developing. UNOTIL to succeed UNMISET as advisory
mission without military component.

2005.assessment:
UNMISET ended May 2005. UNOTIL (UN Office in Timor-Leste) began — police
and judicial advisors only, no military component. Bilateral military
advisors from Australia, Portugal. F-FDTL now responsible for external defense.
Significant reduction from peak UN military presence.

2006.assessment:
International Stabilization Force (ISF) deployed May-June 2006 following
crisis. Australian-led force (~2,500 troops from Australia, NZ, Malaysia,
Portugal). Invited by East Timorese government. ISF parallel to UNMIT
which was authorized August 2006. UNMIT had police component.
Significant invited military force again after reduction period.

2007.assessment:
ISF (~2,500) continuing alongside UNMIT police component. Elections
provided under ISF/UNMIT security. Invited forces maintaining stability
during political transition. F-FDTL and PNTL still needing support.
Bilateral military assistance from Australia and others.

2008.assessment:
ISF continuing after assassination attempt on Ramos-Horta February 2008.
ISF deployed immediately to secure streets of Dili. UNMIT police also
active. Invited international military presence essential for stability.
Rebel Reinado killed — main rebel challenge ended. ISF presence maintained.

2009.assessment:
ISF gradually reducing as security improved. UNMIT police component
continuing. F-FDTL growing more capable. Bilateral military advisors from
Australia, Portugal, others. Transition toward East Timorese security
independence continuing.

2010.assessment:
ISF reduced significantly — approximately 600 troops. UNMIT police
continuing. F-FDTL increasingly capable. Bilateral military advisors.
Security situation stable. Australian-led ISF presence diminishing
toward eventual withdrawal.

2011.assessment:
ISF further reduced — approximately 400 troops. UNMIT completing December
2012. F-FDTL fully operational. Bilateral defense cooperation agreements
with Australia, Portugal. Security situation fully stable. Transition
nearly complete.

2012.assessment:
ISF withdrawn by December 2012. UNMIT also completed December 2012.
Full security independence achieved. F-FDTL and PNTL assumed all
responsibilities. Bilateral defense cooperation advisors (Australia,
Portugal) on invited basis but no combat troops. Full sovereignty in
security sphere achieved.

2013.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. No UN peacekeeping forces. Bilateral defense
cooperation advisors from Australia and Portugal on invited basis.
F-FDTL and PNTL fully responsible for security. No combat foreign military.

2014.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense cooperation with Australia,
Portugal, and others providing advisors and training. F-FDTL professional
force. No combat foreign military presence. Normal defense cooperation
arrangements.

2015.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense advisors from Australia,
Portugal, Malaysia, and others. F-FDTL conducting own training and patrols.
No combat foreign forces. Standard bilateral defense cooperation.

2016.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense advisors. F-FDTL independent.
Timor-Leste also contributing to UN peacekeeping missions — sign of full
security independence. No foreign combat forces. Standard bilateral
defense partnerships.

sources.citation: Defense cooperation; F-FDTL contributing to UN PKO

2017.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Political crisis managed without foreign
military involvement. Elections conducted by East Timorese forces.
Bilateral defense advisors continuing. No foreign combat presence.
Security fully independent.

2018.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense advisors from Australia,
Portugal. Maritime boundary treaty secured — enabling future offshore
security cooperation for Greater Sunrise. No foreign combat forces.

2019.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense cooperation. F-FDTL
professional. PNTL professional. No foreign combat forces. Standard
bilateral defense partnerships. Security fully East Timorese controlled.

2020.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. COVID-19 did not require foreign military
involvement. F-FDTL and PNTL maintaining security. Bilateral defense
partnerships. No foreign combat forces.

2021.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. F-FDTL and PNTL operational. Bilateral
defense partnerships. Timor-Leste contributing to UN PKO. No foreign
combat presence. Full defense sovereignty for 19-year-old state.

2022.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense partnerships. ASEAN accession
in principle will bring new security partnerships. No foreign combat forces.
F-FDTL and PNTL fully operational.

2023.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. ASEAN brings multilateral security frameworks.
Bilateral defense partnerships strong. F-FDTL and PNTL professional
forces. No foreign combat presence.

2024.assessment:
Full security sovereignty. Bilateral defense partnerships. F-FDTL and
PNTL operational. ASEAN membership bringing regional security engagement.
No foreign combat forces. Standard bilateral defense cooperation only.

2025.assessment:
Full security sovereignty expected to continue. Bilateral defense advisors
on invited basis. No foreign combat forces anticipated.

1999.assessment:
Catastrophic displacement. Following independence referendum (August 30,
1999), Indonesian military and militia forced approximately 250,000-300,000
people to flee to West Timor as refugees — roughly 25-30% of entire
population. Additional ~200,000 displaced internally. Total displacement
of ~450,000 of ~850,000 population. Worst humanitarian crisis in East
Timor's modern history. INTERFET deployed September to halt violence.

sources.citation: UNHCR East Timor 1999 crisis; ICG reports

2000.assessment:
Mass returns from West Timor. UNHCR facilitated return of approximately
200,000+ refugees from West Timor during 2000. Some 30,000-50,000 remained
in West Timor camps. September 2000 militia attacks on UNHCR workers in
West Timor camps. Net returnees — population rebuilding. IDPs also returning.

2001.assessment:
Continuing returns from West Timor. Some East Timorese still in West
Timor camps due to fear or pro-Indonesia sentiments. UNHCR facilitating
voluntary return. IDPs from 1999 mostly returned. Pre-independence stability
encouraging returns. Constitutional Assembly elections a positive signal.

2002.assessment:
Mostly stable. Independence May 2002 encouraged last refugee returns.
Some East Timorese who had integrated in West Timor chose not to return.
IDP situation largely resolved. Very small new displacement. Population
rebuilding. UNHCR presence reducing as crisis resolved.

2003.assessment:
Stable displacement situation. Small number of East Timorese remaining
in Indonesia (mainly West Timor) by choice. No significant new displacement.
Population stable. Some minor communal disputes in rural areas creating
very localized movement but no significant displacement flows.

2004.assessment:
Stable. No significant displacement flows. Some internal migration as
people moved toward Dili for employment. Very low international refugee
flows. Some residual 1999 refugees remaining outside East Timor but
numbers small.

2005.assessment:
Stable displacement situation. No significant new displacement. Some internal
migration. Petroleum Fund providing economic stability. No significant
outflows. Building pre-2006 crisis tensions not yet causing displacement.

2006.assessment:
Significant internal displacement from 2006 crisis. Approximately 150,000
people displaced in Dili — roughly 15% of the city's population. This was
mostly internal displacement (IDPs in camps). International outflows
minimal. Crisis was internal/urban, not causing cross-border flows.

2007.assessment:
IDPs gradually returning home from 2006 displacement. 2007 elections
provided political stabilization encouraging returns. By end of 2007
approximately half of 2006 IDPs had returned. Some still in camps.
International outflows minimal. Recovery from 2006 crisis.

2008.assessment:
Further IDP returns from 2006 displacement. February 2008 assassination
attempt on Ramos-Horta caused brief additional displacement in Dili but
quickly resolved. Reinado killed — security improving. Most 2006 IDPs
returned. Very small residual IDP caseload.

2009.assessment:
Stable displacement situation. 2006 IDP caseload largely resolved.
No new significant displacement. Small residual IDP caseload from
2006 events still in transition. Security improving. New displacement
minimal.

2010.assessment:
Stable. All 2006 IDPs returned or resettled. No significant new displacement.
Population movement mainly internal rural-urban migration. Security stable.
No significant cross-border flows.

2011.assessment:
Stable. No displacement. UNMIT completing. Population growing. Rural-
urban migration to Dili continuing. No crisis displacement. International
outflows negligible. Positive demographic stability.

2012.assessment:
Stable. 2012 elections peaceful — no displacement. UNMIT completed.
Full security independence. No crisis displacement. Positive demographic
stability as country consolidated.

2013.assessment:
Stable displacement situation. No significant displacement flows. Population
stable. Internal migration (rural-urban) continuing. East Timor receiving
small number of asylum seekers from Myanmar, Bangladesh passing through.

2014.assessment:
Stable. No displacement. Economy developing with government spending.
Population stable. Rural-urban migration continuing. No crisis displacement.
Small number of transit migrants from other countries.

2015.assessment:
Stable. No significant displacement. Population stable. Small number of
asylum seekers and irregular migrants transiting East Timor. No crisis
events causing displacement. Good security situation.

2016.assessment:
Stable. No displacement. Pre-election stability. Population settled. Labor
migration to Australia and other countries beginning (economic emigration
not displacement). No crisis displacement.

2017.assessment:
Stable displacement despite political crisis. Two election rounds did
not cause any displacement — elections were peaceful. Economic migration
to Australia (Pacific Australia Labour Mobility scheme) growing. No
crisis displacement.

2018.assessment:
Stable. New government. No displacement. Labor migration to Australia
and other Pacific employment scheme countries increasing. This is economic
migration not displacement. No crisis displacement.

2019.assessment:
Stable. No displacement. Labor migration programs continuing. Small
number of asylum seekers in East Timor. No crisis displacement.
Political stability and security maintained.

2020.assessment:
Stable. COVID-19 border closure restricted movement. Labor migration
programs paused. No crisis displacement. Population stable. East Timor
managed COVID without displacement.

2021.assessment:
Stable. Post-COVID recovery. Labor migration resuming. No displacement.
ASEAN accession process. Small asylum seeker/refugee population hosted
(mainly from Myanmar, Sri Lanka, Afghanistan in transit).

2022.assessment:
Stable. No displacement. Labor migration growing (economic emigration).
ASEAN accession in principle. Population stable. Small asylum seeker
population hosted. East Timor a destination for some labor migrants from
Indonesia.

2023.assessment:
Stable. 2023 elections peaceful — no displacement. Labor migration to
Australia and elsewhere increasing. ASEAN accession. Small refugee/
asylum seeker population. No crisis displacement.

2024.assessment:
Stable displacement situation. No crisis displacement. Labor migration
growing as economic safety valve for young population. ASEAN accession
supporting regional mobility. Small hosted refugee/asylum seeker population.

2025.assessment:
Stable displacement expected. Labor migration continuing. ASEAN membership
supporting regional mobility. No anticipated crisis displacement.

1999.assessment:
No sanctions against East Timor. East Timor was the subject of international
sympathy and support, not sanctions. International community imposed limited
sanctions on Indonesia (arms embargo) following post-referendum violence, but
this did not affect East Timor negatively. East Timor received massive
international support.

sources.citation: UN Security Council resolutions on East Timor

2000.assessment:
No sanctions. UNTAET administering territory with full international support.
Heavy international aid flows. No restrictions. East Timor receiving
preferential treatment from international community given its situation.

2001.assessment:
No sanctions. Pre-independence international support strong. Constitutional
Assembly elections internationally supported. International community
uniformly positive toward East Timor's independence process.

2002.assessment:
No sanctions. Independence May 20, 2002 welcomed internationally.
East Timor admitted to UN May 2002. International community fully engaged.
Massive development assistance flowing. No restrictions.

2003.assessment:
No sanctions. International community fully supportive. Development
assistance flowing from World Bank, ADB, bilateral donors. No restrictions.
East Timor recipient of international goodwill.

2004.assessment:
No sanctions. International community engaged. Petroleum Fund established
2005. Timor Sea negotiations with Australia ongoing. International support
for institution-building. No restrictions on trade or finance.

2005.assessment:
No sanctions. Petroleum Fund established. International support for
governance reform. Treaty on Certain Maritime Arrangements in the Timor
Sea (CMATS) signed with Australia. Full international engagement.

2006.assessment:
No sanctions. 2006 crisis attracted international support rather than
sanctions — Australia, New Zealand, Portugal, Malaysia deployed ISF.
International community provided humanitarian support. East Timor viewed
as needing help, not punishment.

2007.assessment:
No sanctions. UNMIT deployed. Elections supported. International community
uniformly positive. Development assistance continued. No restrictions.

2008.assessment:
No sanctions. International support after assassination attempt on
Ramos-Horta. UNMIT and ISF continuing. Full international engagement.
Development assistance flowing.

2009.assessment:
No sanctions. UNMIT ongoing. International development assistance.
Trade relations developing. No restrictions. East Timor member of UN,
engaging with international institutions.

2010.assessment:
No sanctions. UNMIT winding down. International development partnerships.
Petroleum Fund growing. ASEAN membership application being prepared.
Full international engagement. No restrictions.

2011.assessment:
No sanctions. ASEAN membership formal application submitted 2011.
Full international engagement. UN membership in good standing. Development
assistance from multiple bilateral and multilateral sources. No restrictions.

2012.assessment:
No sanctions. UNMIT completed December 2012. International community
praised successful democratic transition and elections. Full sovereignty
achieved. International development partnerships continuing. No restrictions.

2013.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement in post-UNMIT era. ASEAN
accession under consideration. Trade and investment relationships developing.
No restrictions on trade or finance. Positive international standing.

2014.assessment:
No sanctions. CMATS maritime arrangement with Australia — though
this was challenged later. International development partnerships.
No restrictions. Positive international standing.

2015.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. Timor-Leste terminated
CMATS maritime agreement in 2017 (initiating arbitration). ASEAN accession
ongoing. No restrictions. International standing positive.

2016.assessment:
No sanctions. International engagement full. Timor-Leste initiating
arbitration against Australia regarding maritime boundary under UNCLOS.
This was a legitimate legal dispute, not sanctions. International community
supportive.

2017.assessment:
No sanctions. Maritime boundary negotiation with Australia under conciliation.
No trade restrictions. Political crisis domestic only. International
community fully engaged with elections and political transition.

2018.assessment:
No sanctions. Maritime boundary treaty signed with Australia March 2018 —
landmark achievement. ASEAN accession continuing. Full international
engagement. No restrictions.

2019.assessment:
No sanctions. Maritime boundary treaty ratified. International engagement
full. Development partnerships. ASEAN accession process. No restrictions.
Positive international standing.

2020.assessment:
No sanctions. International engagement during COVID-19. Development
assistance including COVID support. No restrictions. Timor-Leste member
of international community in good standing.

2021.assessment:
No sanctions. ASEAN accession progressing. International engagement full.
Development assistance. No restrictions. Positive international standing.

2022.assessment:
No sanctions. International engagement full. Development partnerships.
ASEAN accession continuing. Ramos-Horta returned as President with
international respect. No restrictions.

2023.assessment:
No sanctions. ASEAN admitted Timor-Leste in principle at 2022 summit,
accession ongoing. International engagement very strong. Ramos-Horta
highly active diplomatically. Elections supported internationally.

sources.citation: ASEAN accession in principle; bilateral relations

2024.assessment:
No sanctions. Full international engagement. ASEAN accession pending
formal completion. Development partnerships active. No restrictions.
Positive international standing maintained throughout entire existence.

2025.assessment:
No sanctions expected. Timor-Leste has never faced international sanctions.
ASEAN membership expected to be formalized.

1999.assessment:
Civil liberties severely suppressed under Indonesian occupation followed
by post-referendum violence. Indonesian military and militia campaigns
of killing, displacement, and destruction. No freedom of expression,
assembly, or movement. Survivors in mountains or refugee camps. UNTAET
beginning to restore minimal order by year end. Freedom House rated
East Timor Not Free (CL=6 under Indonesian occupation/crisis).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 1999

2000.assessment:
Civil liberties improving under UNTAET administration. Freedom of
expression and assembly permitted. Cross-border militia raids creating
some insecurity. UNTAET enforcing basic rights. Partly Free rating
emerging. Significant improvement from 1999 but still transitional.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2000

2001.assessment:
Civil liberties improving. Constitutional Assembly elections August 2001
with free campaigns. Press freedom allowed. Freedom of assembly permitted.
UNTAET enforcing basic rights. Partly Free status maintained. Still
transitional limitations from security situation.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2001

2002.assessment:
Civil liberties improving with independence. Freedom of expression,
assembly, and religion broadly respected. Independent media developing.
Some restrictions from FRETILIN-dominated government. Freedom House
improved rating toward Partly Free. Independent East Timorese civil
society forming.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2002

2003.assessment:
Civil liberties at Partly Free level. Freedom of expression maintained.
Independent media operating. Some self-censorship on sensitive topics.
FRETILIN government criticized by civil society. Assembly and religion
free. Women's rights improving. CL=3 (Partly Free) maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2003

2004.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained at Partly Free level. Press freedom functioning.
Civil society active. Some concerns about FRETILIN government's treatment
of critics. Freedom of assembly maintained. Religious freedom high
(predominantly Catholic country). CL=3 (Partly Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2004

2005.assessment:
Civil liberties at Partly Free level. Freedom of expression and assembly.
Civil society growing. Catholic Church influential but not suppressive.
Some concerns about military influence. Press freedom maintained.
Building pre-crisis internal tensions not yet affecting civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005

2006.assessment:
Civil liberties deteriorated during 2006 crisis. Gang violence and military
mutiny created fear environment. IDPs unable to exercise rights freely.
Freedom House downgraded to CL=4. Some journalists faced threats. Government
change (PM Alkatiri resigned) through non-democratic pressure — civil
liberties under stress.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2006

2007.assessment:
Civil liberties recovering. Elections held with generally free campaigns.
Press freedom restored. New government under Ramos-Horta/Gusmao more
pluralistic. UNMIT presence supporting rights. Civil society active.
Freedom House restored to CL=3 (Partly Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2007

2008.assessment:
Civil liberties at Partly Free level. February 2008 assassination attempt
on Ramos-Horta — emergency measures briefly limited some liberties but
quickly restored. Press freedom maintained. Freedom of assembly. Civil
society active. Government broadly respecting civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2008

2009.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained. Freedom of expression, assembly, and religion.
Press freedom functioning. Civil society active. Some concerns about
government use of state resources. UNMIT supporting rights framework.
Partly Free (CL=3) consistent.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2009

2010.assessment:
Civil liberties improving. Press freedom maintained. Civil society vocal.
Freedom of assembly exercised. Some concerns about impunity for 2006
crisis perpetrators. 2012 elections being planned. Women's rights improving
(30% gender quota in parliament). Partly Free (CL=3).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010

2011.assessment:
Civil liberties good for young democracy. Press freedom maintained.
Civil society active. Freedom of assembly. UNMIT completing. 2012
elections planned with confidence. Gender representation improving.
Partly Free (CL=3) maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2011

2012.assessment:
Civil liberties fully functional. Successful free and fair elections in
2012. Press freedom maintained. Civil society engaged in elections. Freedom
of assembly exercised throughout election period. UNMIT completed.
Freedom House rated Timor-Leste Free (CL=3) or Partly Free. Democratic
consolidation evident.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2012

2013.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained. Press freedom. Civil society active. Freedom
of assembly. Some concerns about judicial independence. Government criticism
permitted. Partly Free/Free status consistent. Democratic culture developing.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2013

2014.assessment:
Civil liberties consistent. Press freedom. Civil society vocal on
governance issues. Freedom of assembly and religion. Judiciary improving
slowly. Some concerns about Gusmao government's concentration of power.
Partly Free consistently. Democratic norms holding.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2014

2015.assessment:
Civil liberties at good level for Southeast Asian LDC. Press freedom
generally maintained. Civil society active. Freedom of assembly. Some
self-censorship on topics related to independence-era leaders. Partly
Free (CL=3). ASEAN accession would require maintaining these standards.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2015

2016.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained. Press freedom. Freedom of assembly and religion.
Civil society engaged. Partly Free status consistent (CL=3). Preparing
for 2017 elections. Good civil liberties record for young democracy.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2016

2017.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained during political crisis. Two election rounds
both free and fair. Press freedom maintained. Civil society vocal about
political crisis. Freedom of assembly exercised during campaigns. Political
rights more contested but civil liberties maintained. Partly Free (CL=3).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2017

2018.assessment:
Civil liberties consistent. Political crisis resolved constitutionally.
Press freedom. Civil society active on maritime boundary and fiscal issues.
Freedom of assembly. Partly Free (CL=3) status maintained. Democracy
demonstrated resilience.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2018

2019.assessment:
Civil liberties good. Freedom House rated Timor-Leste Free (upgraded from
Partly Free in some assessments). Press freedom maintained. Civil society
active. Freedom of assembly and religion. Judiciary slowly improving.
Good civil liberties record for 17-year-old democracy.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2019

2020.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained through COVID-19 pandemic. Emergency measures
temporary and proportionate. Press freedom maintained. Civil society
engaged on COVID response. Democracy functioning. Freedom House rating
Free or Partly Free (consistent moderate score).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2020
