2021.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained. Press freedom. Civil society active on
ASEAN accession and fiscal issues. Freedom of assembly. Post-COVID
recovery. Timor-Leste consistently among better-performing democracies
in Southeast Asia for civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2021

2022.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained. Freedom of expression and assembly. Independent
media. Civil society vocal. Gusmao PM again. Democratic culture well-
established. Timor-Leste rated Partly Free (2/4) or Free by Freedom House.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2022

2023.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained through 2023 elections. Free and fair elections
May 2023. Press freedom. Freedom of assembly. Civil society active.
ASEAN accession requires maintaining democratic standards. Timor-Leste
demonstrating 21-year civil liberties track record.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2023

2024.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained. Press freedom. Freedom of assembly and religion.
Civil society engaged. Fiscal challenges not leading to civil liberties
restrictions. Democratic governance with good civil liberties record
for Southeast Asian context.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2024

2025.assessment:
Civil liberties expected to continue at Partly Free/Free level. Democratic
governance established. ASEAN membership encouraging rights maintenance.

1999.assessment:
Resistance leadership (CNRT/FRETILIN) largely unified in independence
struggle. Gusmao (CNRT), Ramos-Horta (CNRM/diplomatic), and FRETILIN
shared independence goal. Post-referendum crisis created common threat.
No East Timorese governing elite yet — UNTAET assuming administration.
Resistance unity under external pressure.

2000.assessment:
Post-independence elite broadly unified around nation-building under UNTAET.
Some tensions between FRETILIN (political party led by Alkatiri) and
Gusmao's more inclusive CNRT. But common framework of independence movement.
Elite working within UNTAET structures.

2001.assessment:
FRETILIN won Constitutional Assembly elections (August 2001) with ~57%.
Gusmao elected as independent/consensus President. FRETILIN under Alkatiri
dominant. Tensions between FRETILIN and other parties. Alkatiri-Gusmao
relationship functional but with underlying tensions.

2002.assessment:
FRETILIN government under PM Alkatiri with President Gusmao (constitutionally
limited role). Alkatiri-Gusmao tensions over governance style and authority.
FRETILIN internal cohesion issues. Opposition parties developing. Elite
broadly functional but cohabitation tensions.

2003.assessment:
Elite functioning but FRETILIN internal divisions growing. Alkatiri
consolidating power within FRETILIN and government. Gusmao more limited
by presidential role. Opposition parties fragmented. Tensions over
governance, patronage, and policy direction.

2004.assessment:
Elite tensions increasing. FRETILIN government under Alkatiri facing
criticism. Gusmao publicly criticizing government performance. Military
(F-FDTL) tensions between east-west soldiers developing. Opposition
parties unifying around anti-FRETILIN stance. Pre-2006 crisis factors building.

2005.assessment:
Elite cohesion declining significantly. Gusmao-Alkatiri conflict becoming
public. Military/police tensions (petition of 591 soldiers organizing).
Opposition parties unified against FRETILIN government. Church-government
tensions. Multiple elite cleavages approaching crisis.

2006.assessment:
Open elite conflict — worst crisis since independence. Military mutiny
(petition of 591 fired soldiers), rebel Reinado leading armed faction,
FRETILIN government losing control. PM Alkatiri forced to resign.
President Gusmao suspending constitution and declaring emergency.
FRETILIN-CNRT elite fracture reached breaking point.

2007.assessment:
Post-crisis realignment. Ramos-Horta elected President (independent).
Gusmao led AMP coalition to government win. FRETILIN went into opposition
for first time. New power configuration with Gusmao as PM, Ramos-Horta
as President. More coherent elite structure than crisis period. Reinado
still rebel but politically isolated.

2008.assessment:
Elite cohesion improving. Reinado killed in assassination attempt on
Ramos-Horta (February 2008) — main rebel challenge ended. AMP coalition
government stable. Gusmao-Ramos-Horta cooperation. FRETILIN in loyal
opposition. Elite structure consolidating around new power configuration.

2009.assessment:
Elite cohesion good. AMP coalition stable under Gusmao. FRETILIN in
organized opposition. Ramos-Horta and Gusmao working together. Security
sector stabilized. Elite consensus around democratic competition rather
than armed conflict.

2010.assessment:
Elite cohesion good. Preparing for 2012 elections. AMP coalition holding.
Some internal coalition tensions among AMP partners. FRETILIN organized
opposition. Democratic competition replacing armed conflict as elite
contestation mode.

2011.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained. 2012 election preparations bringing elite
alignment within coalitions. FRETILIN preparing for electoral competition.
Gusmao's AMP organizing. Democratic transition of power anticipated.
CNRT (Gusmao's new party) dominant.

2012.assessment:
Elite cohesion demonstrated in peaceful power transfer. Taur Matan Ruak
elected President (April), then CNRT (Gusmao) won parliamentary elections
(July), forming ANC coalition. FRETILIN in opposition peacefully. Elite
competing democratically, not violently. Major consolidation milestone.

2013.assessment:
Elite cohesion good. ANC coalition under Gusmao as PM stable. FRETILIN
in loyal opposition. Taur Matan Ruak as President (former military chief,
different background from Gusmao/FRETILIN). Some tensions between President
and PM but managed constitutionally.

2014.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained. Coalition politics functioning. Opposition
organized. Some tensions within ANC coalition but managed. Democratic
competition norms established. Elite consensus around democratic governance.

2015.assessment:
Elite cohesion good. Gusmao government stable. Opposition organized.
2017 elections being prepared. Some concern about Gusmao's personal
dominance in politics — he remains the pivotal figure. Ramos-Horta
less politically active after 2012 (did not seek re-election).

2016.assessment:
Elite cohesion. Preparing for 2017 elections. Gusmao announced not
seeking PM re-election (though remained CNRT leader). Francisco Guterres
(Lu-Olo) leading FRETILIN. Multiple parties campaigning. Democratic
competition culture established.

2017.assessment:
Elite conflict but managed constitutionally. FRETILIN won July 2017
elections but without majority. Lu-Olo elected President. AMP (Gusmao-led
opposition bloc including CNRT) refused to support FRETILIN's PM Ruak's
government program. Constitutional deadlock. Second elections called.
AMP won second elections decisively. Elite competition intense but constitutional.

2018.assessment:
Elite cohesion restored after political crisis. AMP coalition government
formed May 2018 under Gusmao as PM (returning after gap). Lu-Olo as
President (FRETILIN), Gusmao as PM (AMP) — cohabitation. Politically
complex but functioning. Maritime boundary treaty signed.

2019.assessment:
Elite functioning through constitutional tensions. Lu-Olo (FRETILIN President)
refusing to swear in AMP government ministers in some portfolios — significant
institutional tension. But resolved through negotiation. Gusmao dominant
political figure despite constitutional constraints.

2020.assessment:
Elite realignment. AMP coalition collapsed — Gusmao's CNRT withdrew
support from PM Ruak's government. New parliamentary coalition formed
under PM Ruak (Taur Matan Ruak) with FRETILIN and others. Political
realignment through constitutional processes. Gusmao in opposition.

2021.assessment:
Elite functioning in complex coalition politics. PM Ruak continued.
FRETILIN and others in coalition. CNRT in opposition. Political competition
democratic. Preparing for 2023 elections. Fiscal concerns creating common
pressure on all elites.

2022.assessment:
Gusmao returned as PM after CNRT won 2022/2023 elections. Elite
consolidating around Gusmao's return. Some concerns about presidential-PM
tensions again. Democratic competition functioning. Elite broadly unified
on fiscal sustainability challenge.

2023.assessment:
Elite cohesion for fiscal challenges. Parliamentary elections May 2023.
CNRT (Gusmao) won. Political alignment around ASEAN accession and fiscal
sustainability. Elite competition democratic. Ramos-Horta returned as
President (2022). Gusmao-Ramos-Horta cooperation again.

2024.assessment:
Elite cohesion stable. Gusmao PM, Ramos-Horta President — the founding
generation still dominant. FRETILIN in structured opposition. Elite
consensus on need to address fiscal sustainability. Democratic competition
norms well-established. Institutional constraints managing elite conflict.

2025.assessment:
Elite cohesion stable. Succession question (post-Gusmao generation)
emerging as key future issue. Democratic competition norms established.

1999.assessment:
No functioning East Timorese state institutions. Indonesian administration
collapsed after referendum. INTERFET deployed. UNTAET transitional
administration established in late 1999. All governance functions performed
by UN. No courts, legislature, or elected government. Building from zero.

2000.assessment:
UNTAET administering territory. National Consultative Council established.
Early institution-building. No legislature yet but UNTAET Regulation system.
Some East Timorese participating in governance through UNTAET structures.
Institutions being built from scratch.

2001.assessment:
Constitutional Assembly elected August 2001 — first democratic election.
National Consultative Council active. East Timorese increasingly participating
in governance. UNTAET Regulation system functioning. Pre-independence
institutional framework developing.

sources.citation: UNTAET reports; Constitutional Assembly election observation

2002.assessment:
Independence May 20, 2002 — constitution enacted, National Parliament
inaugurated, President Kay Rala Xanana Gusmao elected by parliament,
PM Mari Alkatiri. New state institutions assumed functions from UNTAET.
Very nascent but formally established. UNMISET providing support.
Courts, legislature, and executive all functioning minimally.

2003.assessment:
Institutions consolidating. Parliament functioning. Courts developing
slowly. UNMISET providing legal system support. Bureaucracy growing.
Some institutional capacity challenges — limited trained personnel,
Portuguese/Tetum language issues, limited resources.

2004.assessment:
Institutions developing. Parliament and government functioning. Courts
still very limited capacity. Civil service developing. UNMISET mission
ending May 2005. East Timorese institutions gradually more independent
but still very limited capacity.

2005.assessment:
Institutional capacity growing but fragile. UNOTIL advisory role began.
Parliament functioning with some dysfunction. Internal political tensions
building between FRETILIN government and Gusmao (President). Security
sector tensions foreshadowing 2006 crisis.

2006.assessment:
Institutional functioning severely disrupted by 2006 crisis. PM Alkatiri
resigned. Ramos-Horta became PM. ISF deployed. Parliament continued but
with reduced activity. Courts functioning minimally. Security institutions
partially collapsed during crisis. Government changed mid-year.

sources.citation: ICG East Timor Crisis 2006; Freedom House

2007.assessment:
Institutions recovering. Presidential elections April-May 2007 (Ramos-Horta
won). Parliamentary elections June-July 2007. AMP coalition formed under
Gusmao as PM. Ramos-Horta as President. UNMIT deployed August 2007.
Political realignment through democratic elections — significant institutional
recovery.

2008.assessment:
Institutional functioning improving. February 2008 assassination attempt
on Ramos-Horta was managed constitutionally — institutions held. Parliament
and government continued functioning. Courts developing. UNMIT providing
support. Government recovery from 2006 crisis complete.

2009.assessment:
Institutions developing. Parliament functioning. Government stable.
Judiciary developing with UNMIT support. Civil service growing. Some
institutional capacity challenges remain. Security sector improving.
Electoral commission developing.

2010.assessment:
Institutional functioning improving. Parliament active. Government stable.
UNMIT winding toward 2012 completion. Judiciary still weak but developing.
Election system being prepared for 2012. Civil service capacity growing.
Significant progress from 2002 baseline but still limited.

2011.assessment:
Institutions maturing. Parliament functional. Government stable.
UNMIT completing December 2012. East Timorese institutions preparing
for full independence. Judiciary still limited. Electoral institutions
developed for 2012 elections.

2012.assessment:
Significant institutional milestone. Successful presidential and parliamentary
elections — Taur Matan Ruak elected President (April), ANC coalition won
parliament (July), Gusmao continued as PM. UNMIT mission completed December
2012. Full independent institutional functioning. Democracy consolidating.
Major milestone in institutional development.

sources.citation: UNMIT final reports; election observation missions

2013.assessment:
Post-UNMIT independent institutional functioning. Parliament active. Courts
functional but limited capacity. Government delivering services. Civil
society developing. Some corruption and patronage issues. Democracy functioning
for 11-year-old state.

2014.assessment:
Institutional functioning stable. Parliament passing legislation. Courts
functioning. Government delivering services. Judiciary still weakest
institution. Press freedom generally maintained. Civil society active.
Timor-Leste rated Partly Free by Freedom House.

2015.assessment:
Institutions functioning. Parliament active. Government implementing
development programs. Judiciary capacity improving slowly. Some concerns
about PM Gusmao consolidating power. Freedom House Partly Free. ASEAN
accession application active.

2016.assessment:
Institutions stable. Parliament functioning. Government stable ahead of
2017 elections. Judiciary developing. Civil service improving. Timor-Leste
maintaining functional democratic institutions for 14-year-old state.

2017.assessment:
Institutional functioning tested by political crisis but proved resilient.
Two parliamentary elections held. FRETILIN won July 2017 elections but
AMP opposition rejected President Guterres's (not Gusmao) program.
Political stalemate resolved through constitutional mechanisms, not
institutional collapse. Courts and parliament operating. Democracy stressed
but functioning.

2018.assessment:
Institutional functioning restored after 2017 crisis. New ANC government
formed May 2018. Maritime boundary treaty signed. Parliament passing
legislation. Government implementing programs. Institutions demonstrated
resilience through crisis and recovery.

2019.assessment:
Institutions functioning normally. Parliament and government operational.
Courts functioning. Civil service delivering services. Some capacity
constraints. Fiscal sustainability challenges not threatening institutional
integrity. Timor-Leste rated Free by Freedom House in some years.

2020.assessment:
Institutional functioning maintained through COVID-19 pandemic. Government
managed health response. Parliament continued. Taur Matan Ruak continued
as PM in new coalition. Institutions handling pandemic while managing fiscal
pressure. Democracy maintained.

2021.assessment:
Institutions functioning. ASEAN accession progressing. Government stable.
Parliament active. Judiciary still weakest institution. Civil society vocal
on fiscal sustainability concerns. Democracy functioning.

2022.assessment:
Institutions functioning. Gusmao returned as PM. Government active on
fiscal and development agenda. Parliament passed budgets. Courts functioning.
Timor-Leste demonstrating durable democratic institutions for 20-year-old state.

2023.assessment:
Institutional functioning stable. Parliamentary elections May 2023.
ASEAN accession in principle admitted. Institutions managing fiscal
challenges. Democracy demonstrated durability. Judiciary still weaker
institution. Civil society engaged.

2024.assessment:
Institutions functioning. Government managing fiscal sustainability crisis
through institutional processes. Parliament active. Courts developing.
Freedom House rates Timor-Leste as Free or Partly Free. Democratic
governance consolidated over 22 years.

2025.assessment:
Institutions expected to continue functioning. Democratic governance
established. Fiscal challenges remain but institutional resilience
demonstrated.

1999.assessment:
Catastrophic political violence. Following the independence referendum
(August 30, 1999), Indonesian military (TNI) and pro-Indonesia militia
conducted systematic destruction campaign. Estimated 1,000-2,000+ killed,
thousands more disappeared. 250,000+ forced into West Timor as refugees.
~80% of infrastructure destroyed. INTERFET deployed in late September 1999
to halt violence. Worst violence in East Timor's modern history.

sources.citation: UNHCR East Timor crisis; ICG reports 1999

2000.assessment:
Violence declining significantly under UNTAET administration. Some cross-
border militia incursions from West Timor (UN personnel killed in September
2000 attack). Sporadic attacks by pro-Indonesia militia remnants. UNTAET
peacekeeping force largely maintaining order. Security much improved from 1999.

2001.assessment:
Violence declining further. Constitutional Assembly elections August 2001
conducted without major violence. Cross-border militia incursions reducing.
UNTAET peacekeeping effective. Some localized communal violence. Security
situation substantially improved.

2002.assessment:
Violence at low level around independence. Independence celebrations
May 20, 2002 peaceful. Some localized violence. UNMISET took over from
UNTAET. Some discontent emerging within F-FDTL (military) and PNTL (police)
regarding conditions, ranks, and east-west tensions.

2003.assessment:
Low political violence. UNMISET maintaining security. Some communal and
martial arts group violence. No organized armed opposition. Political
consolidation occurring. Sporadic incidents but no systematic political
violence.

2004.assessment:
Low political violence. UNMISET mission ending May 2005. Internal tensions
in security forces growing (west-east lorosae/loromunu divide in F-FDTL).
Some martial arts gang violence in Dili. Political environment stable.

2005.assessment:
Violence at low level but rising tensions. UNOTIL (UN Office in Timor-
Leste) now in advisory role. Internal F-FDTL tensions increasing. Catholic
Church Bishop Belo-related controversy. Petition of 591 soldiers being
organized. Pre-crisis indicators appearing.

2006.assessment:
Severe political violence. April-May 2006 crisis. F-FDTL soldiers fired
on protesters (killing several). Major Alfredo Reinado deserted and led
rebel faction. Gang violence engulfed Dili. ~150,000 civilians displaced.
Australian-led ISF deployed May 2006. Dozens killed. State effectively
lost control of Dili for period.

2007.assessment:
Violence declining but Reinado rebel faction still active. Elections held
June-July 2007 under tight security — some election-related violence.
UNMIT mission deployed August 2007. Reinado at large in mountains. ISF
presence stabilizing. Post-election violence limited.

2008.assessment:
Violence declining sharply. February 11, 2008 — Reinado killed in failed
assassination attempt on President Ramos-Horta (Ramos-Horta wounded,
survived). This ended the main organized rebel challenge. Remaining
rebel factions surrendered. UNMIT and ISF maintained security.

2009.assessment:
Low political violence. Security situation substantially improved.
UNMIT mandate continuing. Some martial arts gang violence at community
level. No organized armed opposition remaining. Security institutions
improving.

2010.assessment:
Very low political violence. UNMIT winding down toward 2012 completion.
Security situation stable. Some sporadic gang violence. Political tensions
mostly managed through democratic processes. PNTL and F-FDTL more capable.

2011.assessment:
Very low political violence. UNMIT completing. Some localized violence.
Political environment stable ahead of 2012 elections. Security forces
increasingly independent. No significant political violence.

2012.assessment:
Very low political violence. 2012 presidential and parliamentary elections
held peacefully — major milestone. UNMIT mission completed December 2012.
Taur Matan Ruak elected President. ANC coalition formed government.
East Timor demonstrating successful democratic consolidation.

2013.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Post-UNMIT era of full independence. Security
forces managing independently. Some localized community violence. Political
competition through democratic means. No organized armed opposition.

2014.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Country consolidating democratically.
Some sporadic communal violence. No armed challenges to government.
Political violence at extremely low level by regional standards.

2015.assessment:
Very low political violence. Stable democratic state. Security sector
professional. No armed opposition. Isolated communal incidents. Political
competition managed through electoral and parliamentary processes.

2016.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Stable governance. 2017 elections being
prepared. Security situation excellent. No armed opposition. Timor-Leste
among most peaceful states in Southeast Asia by conflict metrics.

2017.assessment:
Very low political violence despite political crisis. Two election rounds.
FRETILIN minority government, then political deadlock. Budget impasse.
All political competition through constitutional democratic processes —
no violence associated with electoral disputes. Functioning democracy.

2018.assessment:
Minimal political violence. New ANC government formed May 2018. Political
crisis resolved. Maritime boundary treaty signed. No armed challenges.
Political competition through democratic means.

2019.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Government stable. No armed challenges.
Some civil protest activity around fiscal concerns but peaceful. Democracy
functioning well by regional standards.

2020.assessment:
Minimal political violence. COVID-19 lockdown measures implemented.
No organized armed opposition. Political competition democratic.
Security sector professional. Timor-Leste one of more peaceful
states in region.

2021.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Post-COVID recovery. ASEAN accession progress.
No armed challenges. Stable governance. Political competition through
institutional channels.

2022.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Gusmao returned as PM. Government stable.
No armed challenges. Some civil society tensions over fiscal policy
but all peaceful. Strong track record of democratic governance.

2023.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Parliamentary elections May 2023 conducted
peacefully. ASEAN accession in principle. Timor-Leste demonstrating
consistently peaceful democratic governance. Fiscal pressure not
translating to political violence.

2024.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Stable democratic governance. Fiscal challenges
significant but political system managing them peacefully. No armed
challenges to government. Security sector professional.

2025.assessment:
Minimal political violence expected to continue. Democratic governance
stable. No armed opposition. Timor-Leste one of the more peaceful
states in Southeast Asia.

1999.assessment:
No government control. Indonesian military and pro-Indonesia militia
conducted systematic destruction following the independence referendum
result (78.5% for independence). Approximately 70-80% of infrastructure
destroyed. Population fled to mountains or West Timor. UNTAET took over
administration in late 1999. No East Timorese government yet existed.

2000.assessment:
UNTAET administered effectively the entire territory. Indonesian military
withdrew from East Timor. Some border areas with West Timor had sporadic
militia incursions. INTERFET (then UNTAET) controlled all major populated
areas. Minor cross-border militia raids from West Timor camps.

2001.assessment:
UNTAET controlled all territory. Constitutional Assembly elections held
August 2001. Cross-border militia incursions declining. East Timorese
police (PNTL) and defense force (F-FDTL) being established. Full territorial
control by UN/UNTAET transitional administration.

2002.assessment:
Full territorial control. Independence May 20, 2002 — East Timorese
government assumed control from UNTAET. UNMISET took over security
support. No significant territorial challenges. New national government
under Gusmao as President, Alkatiri as PM.

2003.assessment:
Full territorial control. Government consolidated across all districts.
UNMISET providing security support. F-FDTL (defense force) and PNTL
(police) developing. No significant armed challenges to state authority.
West Timor border secured.

2004.assessment:
Full territorial control. UNMISET mission ended May 2005. UNOTIL (UN
Office in Timor-Leste) taking over smaller advisory role. Security
institutions maturing. Some intra-military tensions developing (between
western and eastern soldiers in F-FDTL) but no territorial challenges.

2005.assessment:
Full territorial control. UNMISET mission ended, UNOTIL began. Growing
tensions within F-FDTL between easterners (lorosae) and westerners (loromunu).
Petition of 591 soldiers (2006) foreshadowed. Political and security tensions
building but territory still under full government control.

2006.assessment:
Partial control during 2006 crisis. Military mutiny (petition of 591,
dismissed soldiers joining rebel Major Alfredo Reinado) challenged
government authority. Gang violence in Dili beyond police control. ~150,000
IDPs in Dili. Australian-led International Stabilization Force (ISF) deployed
May-June 2006. Government retained nominal control but ISF essential for order.

2007.assessment:
Recovering control. Elections held June-July 2007 under ISF security.
Ramos-Horta elected President; Gusmao formed AMP coalition government.
Reinado still at large in mountains. ISF and UNMIT maintaining security.
Some mountain areas under partial Reinado rebel control. Government
authority re-establishing.

2008.assessment:
Near-full control. February 11, 2008 — Major Reinado killed during
assassination attempt on President Ramos-Horta (Ramos-Horta wounded but
survived). This ended the main rebel challenge. Remaining rebel groups
surrendered. ISF and UNMIT security maintained. Government consolidated
control following Reinado's death.

2009.assessment:
Near-full territorial control. Main rebel challenges ended with Reinado's
death. PNTL (police) and F-FDTL (military) functioning. UNMIT mission
supporting. Remaining rebel groups surrendered or integrated. Security
situation substantially improved.

2010.assessment:
Full territorial control. Security situation stable. UNMIT mission
continuing but winding down. East Timorese security institutions increasingly
capable. Some martial arts group violence at local level but not threatening
government authority. Territory fully administered.

2011.assessment:
Full territorial control. UNMIT mission completing (to end December 2012).
East Timorese security forces increasingly independent. No significant armed
challenges. Political stability established. Territory administered at all
levels.

2012.assessment:
Full territorial control. Successful presidential and parliamentary elections
in 2012 — Taur Matan Ruak elected President, ANC coalition formed.
UNMIT mission completed December 2012. East Timorese security forces
assumed all responsibilities. Major milestone — full independent sovereignty.

2013.assessment:
Full territorial control in post-UNMIT era. East Timorese institutions
operating independently. No significant armed challenges. Some martial
arts group violence at community level but not threatening territorial
control. Political stability maintained.

2014.assessment:
Full territorial control. Government consolidated. East Timorese security
forces established. No armed challenges to state authority. Maritime
boundary negotiations with Australia ongoing but land territory fully controlled.

2015.assessment:
Full territorial control. Stable government. No armed challenges.
Timor-Leste (East Timor) fully administering its territory. Economy
under fiscal pressure from oil price decline but political stability
maintained. ASEAN accession application active.

2016.assessment:
Full territorial control. Stable democratic governance. No significant
challenges to government authority. Security sector functioning well.
Preparing for 2017 elections. Territory fully administered.

2017.assessment:
Full territorial control despite political crisis. Two rounds of parliamentary
elections. FRETILIN minority government, then AMP opposition (led by Gusmao)
rejected FRETILIN president's program. Political deadlock. No budget passed.
All this was constitutional rather than involving armed challenges to territory.

2018.assessment:
Full territorial control. New ANC coalition government formed May 2018.
Political crisis resolved constitutionally. Maritime boundary treaty with
Australia signed. Government reconsolidated. No armed challenges.

2019.assessment:
Full territorial control. Government stable. No armed challenges.
Economy under fiscal pressure. ASEAN accession ongoing. Territory fully
administered with functioning security forces.

2020.assessment:
Full territorial control. COVID-19 managed with border controls. Government
functioning. New political coalition forming (Taur Matan Ruak continued
as PM in new arrangement). No armed challenges to territory.

2021.assessment:
Full territorial control. Government stable. ASEAN accession progress.
Political situation stabilizing after 2017 crisis. No armed challenges.
Territory fully administered.

2022.assessment:
Full territorial control. Gusmao returned as PM. Government stable. No
armed challenges. Territory fully administered. Fiscal pressure from
petroleum revenue decline but political-territorial stability maintained.

2023.assessment:
Full territorial control. ASEAN admitted Timor-Leste in principle in 2023.
Government stable. Fiscal pressure intensifying but territory fully controlled.
Democratic governance functioning.

2024.assessment:
Full territorial control. Government stable. Elections held May 2023 (new
parliamentary elections). No armed challenges. Territory fully administered.
Fiscal sustainability a major challenge but not a territorial control issue.

2025.assessment:
Full territorial control expected to continue. No armed challenges.
Democratic governance functioning. Fiscal challenges not threatening
territorial control.

1999.assessment:
No budget transparency. No East Timorese government existed — UNTAET
transitional administration. Budget processes managed by UN. Citizens
had no access to any fiscal information. Economy destroyed. IBP did not
assess East Timor.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey — East Timor not assessed

2000.assessment:
UNTAET managed fiscal resources from UN trust fund. No public budget
process accessible to East Timorese citizens. Some consultative structures
but not public budgeting. IBP did not assess.

2001.assessment:
Pre-independence budget process under UNTAET. Budget documents not
publicly accessible to East Timorese citizens. Constitutional Assembly
beginning to discuss fiscal frameworks. Pre-independence period, no formal
national budget.

2002.assessment:
First national budget of independent East Timor. Some budget documents
available through UNMISET/government but very limited public accessibility.
Budget system being established from scratch. IBP not yet assessing East
Timor. Very early fiscal transparency.

sources.citation: East Timor Ministry of Finance; IBP

2003.assessment:
Budget transparency very low. Budget documents being prepared with
international technical assistance. Some public access to executive
budget proposal. Citizens still have very limited budget information.
Fiscal systems developing.

sources.citation: Ministry of Finance; World Bank technical assistance

2004.assessment:
Budget transparency slowly developing. Executive budget proposal published.
Parliament reviewing budget. Citizens have some access to budget documents.
IBP first assessed East Timor around this period. Score very low.
Petroleum Fund design discussions creating fiscal transparency debates.

sources.citation: IBP East Timor; Ministry of Finance

2005.assessment:
Budget transparency improving. Petroleum Fund established with explicit
transparency provisions — Bayu-Undan revenues publicly reported.
National budget published. Some in-year reports. IBP score still low
but improving. Petroleum Fund transparency provisions a positive milestone.

sources.citation: IBP East Timor; Petroleum Fund Law

2006.assessment:
Budget transparency disrupted by 2006 crisis. Government collapse/change
(PM Alkatiri resignation) disrupted fiscal processes. Budget crisis
management reduced transparency. Emergency spending not transparently
managed. Political turmoil impacting fiscal transparency.

2007.assessment:
Budget transparency partially restored under new Gusmao government.
Budget published. Petroleum Fund publishing revenues. Some improvement.
IBP assessed Timor-Leste in 2008 survey at ~25/100. Budget process
functioning again.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008; Petroleum Fund

2008.assessment:
IBP OBI score ~26/100 in 2008 survey. Budget documents publicly available.
Executive budget proposal, in-year reports published. Parliament reviewing
budget. Petroleum Fund quarterly transparency reports. Still significant
gaps but improving from very low base. Better than many young democracies.

2009.assessment:
Budget transparency stable to improving. IBP 2010 survey shows Timor-Leste
at ~27/100 — minimal information category. Petroleum Fund revenues very
transparent (public reporting requirement by law). Government budget
documents available. Legislature oversight developing.

2010.assessment:
Budget transparency minimal but stable. IBP 2010 survey ~27/100.
Budget documents published. Petroleum Fund transparency high.
Parliament oversight still limited. Citizens have some access.
Improving trajectory for young democracy.

2011.assessment:
Budget transparency improving. IBP 2012 survey shows Timor-Leste improving.
More budget documents published online. UNMIT completing. Parliament more
active in budget oversight. Petroleum Fund transparency model being praised
internationally.

2012.assessment:
Budget transparency improving significantly. IBP 2012 survey ~35/100.
Online budget portal. Annual budget law published. In-year reports.
Citizens budget document. Legislature budget committee active.
Post-elections government with improved fiscal governance.

2013.assessment:
Budget transparency continuing to improve. Multiple budget documents
published. Petroleum Fund annual reports. In-year monitoring reports.
Legislature oversight improving. Mid-year review published. Citizens
access to budget increasing.

2014.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained at improving level. IBP 2015 survey showed
Timor-Leste at ~38/100. All key budget documents published. Legislature
active in oversight. Petroleum Fund publishing comprehensive annual reports.
Good for 12-year-old democracy.

2015.assessment:
IBP OBI score improved to ~47/100 in 2015 survey — limited information
category improving. Significant progress for 13-year-old state. Budget
portal online. Audit reports published. Legislature budget oversight
functioning. Petroleum Fund model a positive example.

2016.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained. Budget documents published. Legislature
oversight. Petroleum Fund transparent. Pre-2017 election budget processes
functioning well. IBP 2017 survey assessing around this level.

2017.assessment:
Budget transparency declined slightly due to political crisis and budget
impasse. IBP 2017 survey ~45/100. Two election rounds and no approved
budget for extended period reduced transparency quality. Government operating
on interim budget without full transparency. Political crisis impact on
fiscal governance.

2018.assessment:
Budget transparency recovered and improved. New ANC government (from May 2018)
resumed normal budget processes. IBP 2019 survey estimated ~49/100.
Maritime boundary achievement improving investment transparency expectations.
Budget documents published comprehensively.

2019.assessment:
Budget transparency at limited-information level. Budget documents publicly
available. Legislature budget oversight. Petroleum Fund transparent reports.
Audit institution (CFTL) publishing audit reports. IBP ~49/100 maintained.

2020.assessment:
Budget transparency slightly declined. COVID-19 emergency spending reduced
transparency in some areas. Emergency supplementary budgets passed quickly.
Core budget documents still published. IBP 2021 survey ~45/100.

2021.assessment:
Budget transparency at limited level. ASEAN accession process encouraging
transparency improvements. Fiscal pressure from declining petroleum revenues
making budget transparency more politically sensitive. IBP ~42/100.
Legislature active in oversight.

2022.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained. Gusmao returned as PM. Budget documents
published. Legislature review. Petroleum Fund quarterly reports.
Fiscal challenge transparency — Petroleum Fund declining widely reported.
IBP ~45/100.

2023.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained despite fiscal crisis. 2023 elections
fiscal accountability an issue. Budget documents published. Petroleum
Fund sustainability crisis transparency — fiscal cliff widely discussed.
IBP ~45/100. Pressure for better transparency as crisis deepens.

2024.assessment:
Budget transparency at limited level. Fiscal crisis from petroleum revenue
decline creating pressure on budget transparency. Petroleum Fund declining
disclosed publicly. Budget documents published. Legislature review.
Some reduction from fiscal sustainability concerns.

2025.assessment:
Budget transparency at limited level expected. Fiscal crisis may motivate
improved transparency as accountability increases. ASEAN membership
supporting transparency norms.

1999.assessment: No functioning legal framework. Indonesian legal system collapsed post-referendum. TNI/militia rampage destroyed judicial infrastructure. INTERFET established basic security but no civilian legal system existed. UNTAET Regulation 1999/1 established transitional administration in November 1999.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. East Timor: Justice Abandoned. 2000.

2000.assessment: Minimal legal framework under UNTAET administration. UNTAET regulations served as de facto law. East Timorese judges and lawyers extremely scarce; UN international judges filled gap. District courts began operating with significant backlog. Indonesian-era laws applied where not inconsistent with UNTAET regulations.

sources.citation: Judicial System Monitoring Programme (JSMP). 2001 Annual Report.
Chesterman, S. East Timor in Transition. IPA. 2001.

2001.assessment: Nascent legal system under UNTAET. Constituent Assembly elected August 2001; constitution drafting began. UNTAET regulations codified into accessible format. Courts operating but severely capacity-constrained. No systematic legal publication mechanism.

sources.citation: Stahn, C. The Law and Practice of International Territorial Administration. 2008.

2002.assessment: Constitution adopted March 2002; independence May 20, 2002. Constitution established Portuguese-influenced civil law system. Jornalismo Republica (official gazette) established. Basic legal framework published but judicial capacity extremely limited. UNMISET supported justice sector. Laws sporadically available in Portuguese and Tetum.

sources.citation: Constitution of the Democratic Republic of Timor-Leste (2002)
UNMISET Justice Sector Support Programme reports

2003.assessment: Legal system developing under UNMISET support. Jornalismo Republica publishing laws in Portuguese. Petroleum Law framework drafted. Courts operating but chronically under-resourced. Significant gap between law on paper and enforcement capacity. National Parliament enacting basic legislation.

sources.citation: World Bank. Timor-Leste Public Expenditure Review. 2003.

2004.assessment: UNOTIL transition period. National legislative framework gradually expanding. Timor-Leste Law Reform Commission established. Laws published in Jornalismo Republica but not systematically indexed or accessible online. Arbitration enforcement weak. Criminal procedure code enacted.

2005.assessment: Petroleum Law enacted 2005; Petroleum Fund Law enacted. Legal framework for oil revenues formally established. Civil Code still not enacted (adopted 2011). Land law reform ongoing and contentious. Courts functioning with significant delays. Laws published in official gazette; limited public accessibility.

sources.citation: Law No. 13/2005 on the Petroleum Fund

2006.assessment: 2006 political and security crisis severely disrupted judicial functioning. Courts unable to function in crisis period. Impunity concerns for violence during crisis. UNMIT established with justice component. Legal framework on paper remained but enforcement collapsed during crisis months.

sources.citation: UNMIT. Report on Human Rights and Justice Sector 2006.
UN Special Representative. Reports on Timor-Leste 2006.

2007.assessment: Post-crisis recovery; elections held March-June 2007. Courts resuming normal operations. UNMIT justice component supporting capacity building. 2006 violence cases prosecuted with mixed results. Legal framework increasingly published but limited online access. Serious Crimes process continued addressing 1999 atrocities.

sources.citation: UNMIT. Human Rights and Transitional Justice Section reports 2007.
International Crisis Group. Timor-Leste: Security Sector Reform. 2008.

2008.assessment: February 2008 assassination attempt on President Ramos-Horta (survived); Alfredo Reinado killed. Laloran incident led to state of emergency declared. Courts handled cases arising from February events. Legal framework improving; Parliament active in legislation. Laws accessible through official gazette but no comprehensive online database.

2009.assessment: Stability improving. Penal Code enacted (Law 35/2009). Criminal Procedure Code in use. Legal framework expanding with new legislation. UNMIT justice sector support continuing. Courts improving throughput but significant backlogs remain. Freedom of Information legislation not yet enacted.

sources.citation: UNMIT. Progress Report on Justice Sector 2009.

2010.assessment: Legal framework maturing. Anti-Corruption Commission (CAC) operational. Civil Code still pending. Courts functioning with reduced UN support. WJP Rule of Law Index not yet covering Timor-Leste. Laws published in Jornalismo Republica but limited digital access. Transitional justice mechanisms winding down.

2011.assessment: First WJP Rule of Law Index coverage for Timor-Leste. Score 0.44/1.00, ranking mid-lower range globally. Civil Code enacted 2011 (Law 10/2011) — major milestone. Laws increasingly accessible through official gazette. Enforcement capacity improving but corruption and impunity remain concerns. Limited FOI framework.

sources.citation: UNMIT. Final Report on Rule of Law 2011.

2012.assessment: UNMIT mission completed December 2012. Elections held successfully; peaceful transfer of power. Legal framework consolidated with Civil Code in force. Courts increasingly independent. WJP score improved to ~0.46. FOI legislation still absent. Online legal database (legalinfo.mj.gov.tl) being developed.

sources.citation: UNMIT. End of Mission Report. December 2012.

2013.assessment: Post-UNMIT period; legal system operating independently. WJP score approximately 0.45. Commercial law framework improving; land law still contested. Courts handling increased caseload. Anti-corruption cases filed but enforcement inconsistent. Legal information system (legalinfo) launched with searchable database.

2014.assessment: Legal framework stable. WJP score ~0.46. Laws accessible via legalinfo.mj.gov.tl in Portuguese and some in Tetum. Parliamentary legislation increasingly published promptly. Customary law (tara bandu) recognition in legislation. Land Law still unresolved. Commercial dispute resolution improving.

sources.citation: Ministry of Justice. Legal Information System annual report 2014.

2015.assessment: WJP score ~0.47; incremental improvement. Land Law enacted 2017 (after years of delay). Anti-money laundering framework enacted. Courts handling growing commercial caseload. Law faculty at UNTL producing graduates improving national legal capacity. FOI law still absent but ombudsman provedoria active.

sources.citation: Provedoria de Direitos Humanos e Justica. Annual Report 2015.

2016.assessment: WJP score ~0.46, slight dip. Political tensions over budget (government dissolution January 2017). Courts operational. Legal aid services under development. Laws published in official gazette and legalinfo. Enforcement gap remains wide in rural areas where customary justice prevails.

2017.assessment: Government dissolved January 2017; elections July 2017. Caretaker government limited legislative activity. WJP score declined to ~0.44. Land Law finally enacted (Law 13/2017). Dispute resolution for land claims improving. Political deadlock affected institutional functioning including courts budget.

2018.assessment: Continued political crisis; government formation delayed. Maritime boundary treaty signed with Australia March 2018. WJP score ~0.44. Land Law implementing regulations under development. Courts managing without full government; judicial independence concerns during budget uncertainty.

2019.assessment: Government of National Unity (AMP coalition) functioning. Legal system recovering from political instability. WJP score recovered to ~0.45. Land registration activities under new Land Law. E-government initiatives begun improving legal accessibility. Courts fully operational.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 state of emergency declared March 2020; parliament suspended temporarily. WJP score ~0.45. Emergency regulations enacted by decree — rule by decree concerns during pandemic. Courts operated with reduced capacity. Digital legal access improved enabling remote access to official gazette.

2021.assessment: Political transition: Taur Matan Ruak government succeeded by caretaker then new coalition. WJP score stable ~0.45. COVID emergency orders winding down. Legal framework stable; courts functioning. FOI law still not enacted (a notable gap). Anti-corruption enforcement remains inconsistent.

2022.assessment: WJP score ~0.46, modest improvement. Elections held May 2022; Fretilin won but coalition building required. Xanana Gusmao formed new government late 2022. ASEAN membership in principle approved November 2022. Legal harmonization with ASEAN norms beginning.

2023.assessment: WJP score ~0.46. Xanana Gusmao government implementing legal reforms ahead of ASEAN accession. Commercial courts improving. Online legal information system updated. ASEAN legal harmonization requirements driving legislative agenda. Anti-corruption enforcement improving with new leadership at CAC.

2024.assessment: WJP score approximately 0.46 (estimated based on trend). Legal framework broadly stable. Tasi Mane project and ASEAN accession preparations driving regulatory reform. E-government systems improving legal access. FOI legislation remains absent — a significant transparency gap. Courts functioning adequately.

sources.citation: Ministry of Justice. 2024 Legal Reform Update.

2025.assessment: Legal framework continuing gradual improvement. ASEAN accession legal harmonization process ongoing. Commercial and investment law reform driven by need to attract investment for Tasi Mane gas project. FOI legislation still absent. Legal aid system expanding. Estimated WJP score ~0.46-0.47.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2024 (most recent available)
Ministry of Justice. Legal Reform Plan 2025.

1999.assessment: No functioning press in East Timor during the 1999 crisis. Indonesian military and militia destroyed media infrastructure post-referendum. Foreign journalists were targeted; several killed or expelled. INTERFET arrival in September 1999 allowed limited international media access. No independent local press existed.

sources.citation: Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ). East Timor 1999 reports.
RSF. East Timor press freedom emergency reports 1999.
Human Rights Watch. East Timor: Broken Promises 1999.

2000.assessment: Under UNTAET administration; press freedom gradually recovering. UNTAET established Radio UNTAET and Televisao Timor-Leste. Community radio stations beginning. Some local press emerging. UNTAET regulations provided basic press freedom framework. International journalists operating with INTERFET/UNTAET access. Local journalists beginning training.

2001.assessment: Pre-independence; UNTAET media environment relatively open. Radio Falintil/RTTL broadcasting. Suara Timor Lorosae newspaper established. Constituent Assembly campaign coverage allowed. Constitution drafting process covered by local and international media. Press freedom improving from crisis nadir.

2002.assessment: First year of independence; press freedom relatively good for a new state. Constitution guaranteed press freedom. Suara Timor Lorosae and Timor Post operating. Radio RTTL as public broadcaster. No systematic censorship. Some self-censorship regarding senior political figures (Xanana, Ramos-Horta). RSF ranked Timor-Leste favourably in first coverage.

sources.citation: Constitution of Timor-Leste Article 40-41 (freedom of expression)

2003.assessment: Press freedom environment broadly positive. Multiple newspapers operating (Suara Timor Lorosae, Timor Post, Semanario). Radio stations increasing including community radio. Some incidents of intimidation of journalists. Limited reach to rural areas. Portuguese/Tetum language barrier limited audience. RSF ranked mid-range globally.

2004.assessment: Press freedom generally maintained. RTTL as state broadcaster; commercial media developing. Some political pressure on journalists covering security issues. Media sustainability concerns: limited advertising market, low literacy rates constraining print. RSF continued to rank Timor-Leste relatively positively.

2005.assessment: Media environment stable but fragile. Television Timor-Leste (TVTL) main broadcaster. Print media circulation limited. Catholic Church's Radio Timor Kmanek providing community information. Journalists lacked formal training; self-censorship present. No cases of journalist imprisonment but economic pressure significant. RSF ranked mid-range.

2006.assessment: 2006 crisis severely impacted press freedom. TVTL and newspaper offices targeted by mobs. Journalists threatened and attacked covering the crisis. Media infrastructure damaged. State of emergency restrictions on movement impeded coverage. Self-censorship increased significantly. RSF ranking declined. Some foreign media expelled.

sources.citation: CPJ. East Timor 2006: Journalists under threat during crisis.
Human Rights Watch. Timor-Leste 2006 Human Rights Report.

2007.assessment: Recovery from 2006 crisis. Elections covered with reasonable freedom. Media outlets resumed full operation. TVTL, Suara Timor Lorosae, Timor Post all operational. Election coverage contested but diverse. Some harassment of journalists by political supporters. RSF ranking recovering from 2006 decline.

sources.citation: International Media Support. Timor-Leste election media 2007.

2008.assessment: February 2008 assassination attempt; state of emergency (Feb-Apr 2008) restricted movement but press continued operating. State of emergency imposed some coverage limitations near military operations. Press Law (Law 7/2008) enacted — broadly protective of press freedom. Criminal defamation provisions remained concerning. RSF ranked mid-range.

2009.assessment: Press environment improving post-state of emergency. Press Law framework in place. Multiple newspapers and radio stations operating. Television Timor-Leste national broadcaster. Internet access beginning to expand; online media embryonic. Some defamation suits filed by officials against journalists. RSF improvement.

2010.assessment: Media environment stable and relatively free. New media outlets established. Journalists receiving international training. Online news beginning to develop. RSF ranked Timor-Leste in satisfactory range — notably better than most Southeast Asian neighbours. Criminal defamation provisions retained in Penal Code 2009 remain concern.

2011.assessment: RSF ranked Timor-Leste 94th out of 179 countries (2011-2012 index) — score ~63/100 inverted. Good for Southeast Asia. Press Law framework functioning. Media outlet diversity increased. RTTL (public), TVTL, multiple newspapers, community radio. Internet freedom broad. Some political pressure on state broadcaster.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom of the Press 2011.

2012.assessment: RSF score approximately 64 (inverted). Ranked best in Southeast Asia for press freedom. Elections held with diverse media coverage. UNMIT completed; media now operating without UN support. Some journalist self-censorship regarding Xanana Gusmao government. Online media growing. State broadcaster independence questions.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom of the Press 2012.

2013.assessment: RSF score ~62 (inverted); slight decline. Online media growing; social media adoption accelerating. Timorese Journalist Association active. Some incidents of intimidation by security forces. Criminal defamation cases filed against journalists. RSF noted structural fragility of media sector despite relative freedom.

2014.assessment: RSF score ~61 (inverted). Media environment broadly free. Internet access expanding; social media primary news platform for urban youth. Some defamation cases using Penal Code provisions. State broadcaster independence concerns. Media sector economically fragile with small advertising market. RSF noted as relatively free.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom of the Press 2014.

2015.assessment: RSF score ~60 (inverted). RSF 2016 index noted some concerns about legal threats to journalists. Media landscape: 5+ newspapers, 30+ radio stations, 2 TV channels, growing online media. Press Law broadly protective. Some self-censorship on topics of elite politics and security. Overall environment satisfactory.

2016.assessment: RSF score ~58 (inverted). Slight deterioration. Political tensions around budget disputes and government dissolution began affecting media. Some journalists received threats during political crisis period. Online media proliferating; social media disinformation concerns emerging. Structural freedom maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom on the Net Timor-Leste 2016.

2017.assessment: RSF score ~57 (inverted). Government dissolved January 2017; elections July 2017. Media covered elections with diversity. Some polarization between pro-Fretilin and pro-CNRT/AMP media. Caretaker government period no new legislation. Online news sites proliferating. Freedom of Information law still absent.

sources.citation: Carter Center. Timor-Leste 2017 election media report.

2018.assessment: RSF score ~56 (inverted). Continued political crisis with government formation deadlock. Media navigating political tensions. Maritime boundary treaty coverage allowed. Online media growing; social media dominant platform. Journalists report some difficulty accessing government information during caretaker period.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom of the Press 2018.

2019.assessment: RSF score ~55 (inverted). AMP government (Xanana) operational. Media environment broadly free but some editorial pressure on state broadcaster. Online media dominant; social media disinformation spreading. Some journalists face legal threats using Criminal Defamation provisions (Penal Code Art. 285). Tabloid journalism increasing.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom on the Net 2019.

2020.assessment: RSF score ~54 (inverted). COVID-19 pandemic: state of emergency affected journalist access. Government press conferences limited during emergency. Online media maintained coverage. Some pandemic disinformation spread on social media. RSF noted COVID restrictions as temporary press freedom concern. Overall environment maintained.

sources.citation: Timor-Leste state of emergency media provisions 2020.

2021.assessment: RSF score ~53 (inverted). Post-pandemic recovery. Political transition (new government 2022). Media outlet consolidation continues due to economic pressure. Online news dominant. Journalists Union of Timor-Leste (JTKTL) active in press freedom advocacy. Some cases of social media intimidation of journalists. RSF continued satisfactory ranking.

2022.assessment: RSF score ~52 (inverted). Elections held May 2022; political transition to Xanana government December 2022. Media covered elections broadly freely. Online media dominant; print declining. ASEAN accession process beginning to encourage media law review. No journalist imprisonments. Freedom of Information law still absent.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom of the Press 2022.

2023.assessment: RSF score ~51 (inverted); RSF 2024 index. Xanana government; some concerns about concentration of media ownership. Online media with strong social media presence. Journalists covering government corruption face some pressure. ASEAN accession driving media law discussion. Criminal defamation still on books. Broadly satisfactory environment.

sources.citation: Freedom House. Freedom of the Press 2023.

2024.assessment: RSF score estimated ~50 (inverted). Media environment broadly free. ASEAN accession requirements encouraging media law review including potential FOI legislation. Social media dominant. Some incidents of journalist intimidation by officials. Economic pressure on independent media outlets. Criminal defamation provisions remain. Satisfactory overall.

2025.assessment: Press environment projected to remain broadly satisfactory. ASEAN accession preparations may include media law review. FOI legislation potentially moving forward. Online and social media dominant. Structural fragility of media sector (economic sustainability) remains. Estimated RSF inverted score ~49-51.

1999.assessment: No national statistical system existed. Indonesian statistical apparatus (BPS) destroyed or withdrawn. Population data from Indonesian period. No functioning National Statistics Office. UNTAET established basic administrative records. No meaningful statistical capacity.

2000.assessment: Nascent statistical capacity under UNTAET. First population surveys begun by UNTAET. No National Statistics Office. World Bank and UN agencies conducting limited sectoral surveys. Data collection extremely limited. UNTAET administrative data primary source.

sources.citation: World Bank. Timor-Leste Country Data Profile 2001.

2001.assessment: Population and Housing Census 2001 conducted — first major statistical exercise under UNTAET. National Directorate of Statistics being established. Very limited but growing data collection. World Bank assisting with statistical capacity development. GDP estimates extremely rough.

sources.citation: UNTAET. National Directorate of Statistics establishment 2001.

2002.assessment: National Directorate of Statistics (NDS-DNSE) established under Ministry of Finance post-independence. Very limited statistical publications. GDP data unreliable. No systematic economic surveys. World Bank and ADB providing technical assistance. Demographic data from 2001 census.

sources.citation: Timor-Leste National Directorate of Statistics establishment documents 2002.

2003.assessment: Statistical capacity slowly developing. National accounts estimates improving with WB/IMF assistance. Timor-Leste Living Standards Measurement Survey begun. CPI (consumer price index) compilation started. Limited labor force data. GDP revisions frequent. Statistical publications irregular.

2004.assessment: First WB SCI coverage for Timor-Leste; score approximately 30/100. Very poor statistical capacity by international standards. National accounts improving; poverty data emerging from TLSS. CPI compiled regularly. No agricultural census. Population projections from 2001 census. Statistical publications in Portuguese; limited Tetum.

2005.assessment: WB SCI ~32. Slow improvement. Petroleum sector statistics initiated following Petroleum Fund establishment. DNSE producing regular statistical bulletins. National accounts revisions incorporating oil sector. Survey capacity improving with donor support (ADB, World Bank, USAID). First international comparisons available.

sources.citation: Timor-Leste Petroleum Fund authority statistical reports 2005.

2006.assessment: WB SCI ~31; slight dip from 2006 crisis. DNSE operations partially disrupted by security crisis. Statistical staff affected by displacement. Survey activities suspended during crisis period. Data collection capacity temporarily reduced. Recovery by end of year but output delayed.

2007.assessment: WB SCI recovered to ~35. Post-crisis recovery. National Population and Housing Census 2010 preparation begun. DNSE reorganized and strengthened. GDP revisions incorporating better petroleum sector data. Import/export data improving. Demographic and Health Survey planned. Survey capacity building with UNFPA support.

2008.assessment: WB SCI ~38. Continuing improvement. Timor-Leste Demographic and Health Survey (TLDHS) conducted. Petroleum Fund reporting providing high-quality sector statistics. National accounts methodology improving. DNSE received CARICOM/UN statistical training. Data dissemination via website beginning. GDP revisions narrowing uncertainty intervals.

2009.assessment: WB SCI reached ~40 (moderate threshold). Consistent improvement trend. National agricultural survey conducted. Trade statistics improving. IMF data standards (GDDS) participation begun. DNSE producing quarterly GDP estimates. Statistical yearbook increasingly comprehensive. Data portal development started.

2010.assessment: WB SCI ~43. Population and Housing Census 2010 conducted. Census provides comprehensive demographic baseline. DNSE significantly strengthened by census experience. GDP methodology revised. Labor Force Survey conducted. Statistical yearbook comprehensive. Online data portal launched.

2011.assessment: WB SCI ~45. Post-census data release and analysis. GDP revised with new population benchmarks. IMF Data Standards subscription improving. Statistical yearbook published online. DNSE producing regular CPI, trade, and national accounts releases. Petroleum sector statistics world-class via Petroleum Fund reporting.

2012.assessment: WB SCI ~47. DNSE increasingly professional. Statistical Master Plan developed. Data releases more regular and timely. ASEAN statistical cooperation engagement beginning. ADB Statistical Capacity Building Program supporting. Trade and BOP statistics improving. Poverty data from TLSS 2011 published.

sources.citation: ADB. Statistical Capacity Building Programme Timor-Leste 2012.

2013.assessment: WB SCI ~48. Continued improvement. SDG preparatory statistical work begun. Business registration statistics improved. Agricultural census initiated. DNSE website providing online data access. Statistical products increasingly published in Tetum improving public access. National Strategy for Development Statistics progressing.

sources.citation: PARIS21. Timor-Leste National Strategy for Development Statistics.

2014.assessment: WB SCI ~49. DHS 2016 preparation. GDP methodology review incorporating informal sector better. IMF GDDS compliance improving. Regular statistical releases on CPI, trade, national accounts, population. Statistical publications available online in Portuguese and Tetum. Still below 50 globally but improving.

2015.assessment: WB SCI ~50; crossed into moderate-to-good range. SDG monitoring framework statistical requirements driving capacity building. National Statistics Organization Act under consideration. Survey capacity improving. Micro-data archive established. Data accessibility portal upgraded. ASEAN statistical engagement increasing.

sources.citation: UN Statistics Division. SDG indicator baseline work Timor-Leste 2015.

2016.assessment: WB SCI ~51. DHS 2016 conducted providing updated health and demographic statistics. SDG monitoring data collection underway. National statistics law drafted. Budget transparency linked to statistical reporting improving. Political crisis did not significantly affect statistical operations.

sources.citation: SDG Voluntary National Review Timor-Leste preparation 2016.

2017.assessment: WB SCI ~50; slight dip. Political crisis (two elections, caretaker government) affected statistical resource allocation. DHS 2016 report published. Government dissolution created uncertainty in budget for DNSE activities. Statistical operations maintained at reduced pace. SDG data compilation continued.

2018.assessment: WB SCI ~51. Recovery from 2017-2018 political instability. Population and Housing Census 2015 data still being disseminated. National Statistics Office Law enacted 2018 formalizing DNSE as General Directorate for Statistics (DGE). Upgraded institutional status improving capacity. SDG monitoring indicators published.

2019.assessment: WB SCI ~52. DGE (formerly DNSE) consolidated. Voluntary National Review on SDGs submitted to HLPF 2019. Statistical data portal improved. Agriculture census data released. Timor-Leste Poverty and Living Standards Survey (TLSS) 2019 conducted. IMF data standards improving. ASEAN statistical harmonization starting.

2020.assessment: WB SCI ~53 — final year of SCI publication (WB discontinued SCI after 2020, replaced by Statistical Performance Indicators). COVID-19 disrupted some survey activities. Population Census 2021 postponed. Administrative data continued. Digital data collection piloted. DGE maintained core statistical outputs during pandemic.

sources.citation: World Bank. Statistical Performance Indicators replacement framework 2021.
PARIS21. Statistics in Times of Crisis Timor-Leste 2020.

2021.assessment: WB SCI discontinued; WB Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI) introduced. SPI 2021 for Timor-Leste approximately 40-45 on 0-100 scale (different methodology). Population Census 2021 conducted. SDG monitoring ongoing. DGE operational with ASEAN statistical harmonization activities. COVID impact on survey activities waning.

2022.assessment: WB SPI for Timor-Leste in 2022 approximately 42-47 (moderate range). 2021 Census data published. ASEAN accession statistical requirements driving harmonization. DGE active. SDG VNR second cycle preparation. Economic data improving with oil sector restructuring. Trade and national accounts data quality improving.

2023.assessment: Statistical capacity continuing development under ASEAN accession framework. SPI estimated in moderate range. SDG monitoring data increasingly comprehensive. National Statistics Plan 2023-2027 enacted. Digital statistical systems upgrading. Microdata access expanding. ASEAN statistical harmonization requirements met for most core indicators.

2024.assessment: Statistical capacity estimated in moderate range based on trend. ASEAN accession requirements continuing to drive improvements. Labour Force Survey 2024 conducted. National accounts methodology updated incorporating better petroleum sector measurement. Digital data dissemination improving public access. SPI estimated moderate range.

2025.assessment: Statistical capacity projected to continue moderate improvement trajectory. ASEAN full accession expected; statistical harmonization requirements will drive further gains. DGE with National Statistics Plan 2023-2027 implementation ongoing. Tasi Mane project environmental and economic statistics developing. Estimated in moderate range, improving.

sources.citation: DGE. National Statistics Plan 2023-2027 implementation report.
ASEAN statistical requirements for full membership 2025.
